<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — INTRODUCTION TO DEEP FEEDFORWARD NETWORKS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deep Feedforward Network** (also called a **Multilayer Perceptron, MLP**, or **Fully-Connected Deep Network**) is a mathematical composition of parametric, differentiable functions arranged in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) that maps a fixed-dimensional input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{in}}}$ to an output vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}}}$.

Formally, an $L$-layer feedforward network implements the mapping:

$$f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = f^{(L)} \circ f^{(L-1)} \circ \cdots \circ f^{(2)} \circ f^{(1)}(\mathbf{x})$$

where each layer transformation $f^{(\ell)}$ is of the form:

$$f^{(\ell)}(\mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)}) = \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)} \left( \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \right)$$

Here:
- $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\ell} \times d_{\ell-1}}$ is the **weight matrix** of layer $\ell$.
- $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\ell}}$ is the **bias vector**.
- $\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)}$ is the **element-wise activation function**.
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}, \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}\}_{\ell=1}^{L}$ is the parameter set.
- The "depth" refers to the number of layers $L$, while the "width" refers to the dimensionality $d_{\ell}$ of the hidden representations $\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)}$.

The term *"feedforward"* indicates the absence of feedback connections — information flows strictly from input $\mathbf{x}$ through intermediate layers to the output $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$, without any recurrent loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Deep feedforward networks form the *foundational archetype* upon which all modern deep architectures (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, GANs) are constructed. Mastery of this topic is a prerequisite for Module 3 (Regularization & Optimization) and Module 4 (CNNs).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hiring Committee**
>
> Imagine you are screening job applications. Your **input layer** is a stack of 1,000 resumes, each described by 10 features (GPA, experience, skills...). A **single neuron in the first hidden layer** might act like a junior recruiter who looks at two features at a time and asks: *"Does this candidate have enough experience AND communication skills?"* If yes, they pass a signal forward; if no, they remain silent.
>
> A **second hidden layer** of senior recruiters then looks at the outputs of the junior layer and asks higher-order questions: *"Does the candidate also show leadership AND creativity?"*
>
> Finally, an **output layer manager** makes the final hiring decision based on the senior assessments.
>
> Each "recruiter" (neuron) has learnable *weights* (how much to value each feature), a *bias* (their personal baseline tendency), and an *activation function* (whether they choose to speak up or stay silent).
>
> During training, the company receives feedback on every wrong hire and adjusts each recruiter's priorities — this feedback loop is **gradient-based learning**.

This is precisely what a deep feedforward network does: it learns a hierarchy of increasingly abstract features through stacked nonlinear transformations.

---

### 1.3 Why "Deep" Feedforward Networks?

A **shallow network** (single hidden layer) can, in principle, approximate any continuous function on a compact domain — this is the celebrated **Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT)**. However:

- **Depth provides *exponential* representational efficiency** for many functions (Montúfar et al., 2014).
- Deep hierarchies naturally mirror the compositional structure of real-world data (pixels → edges → motifs → objects → scenes).
- Empirical performance on tasks like image recognition, NLP, and speech scales favorably with depth.

---

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Neuron / Unit** | Computational node that computes a weighted sum and applies a nonlinearity. |
| **Layer** | A vector of neurons operating in parallel at the same depth. |
| **Hidden Layer** | Any layer between the input and output (not directly observed). |
| **Activation Function** | Nonlinearity $\sigma(\cdot)$ enabling universal approximation. |
| **Forward Pass / Propagation** | Computation of $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ from $\mathbf{x}$ by passing through layers. |
| **Loss Function** | Scalar measure $\mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$ of prediction error. |
| **Parameters ($\boldsymbol{\theta}$)** | The learnable weights and biases. |
| **Architecture** | The choice of $L$ (depth), $\{d_{\ell}\}$ (widths), and activation functions. |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy

A deep feedforward network decomposes into three functional blocks:

1. **Input Layer** — Holds the raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_0}$. No computation is performed here; it is merely the network's "sensor array".
2. **Hidden Layers** — A sequence of $L-1$ layers (typically) where the bulk of representation learning occurs. Each hidden layer applies an affine transformation followed by a nonlinear activation.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces the final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$. The choice of activation here is **task-dependent**:
   - **Linear**: $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(L)}\mathbf{h}^{(L-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L)}$ → regression.
   - **Sigmoid**: $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ → binary classification.
   - **Softmax**: $\hat{y}_i = \tfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ → multiclass classification.

---

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — How a Feedforward Network Computes

### Step 1: Parameter Initialization
- Weights are typically initialized using **Xavier/Glorot** ($\mathcal{U}[-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}}, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}}]$) or **He** initialization ($\mathcal{N}(0, \tfrac{2}{d})$) to maintain signal variance across layers.

### Step 2: Pre-activation (Affine Transformation)
For layer $\ell$:
$$\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$
This computes a linear combination of the previous layer's activations.

### Step 3: Activation (Nonlinearity)
$$\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)}(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$$
The nonlinearity enables the network to learn non-linear decision boundaries.

### Step 4: Loss Computation
At the output:
$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$$
Common losses include MSE for regression and cross-entropy for classification.

### Step 5: Backpropagation (covered in Section 3)
Gradients $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} \mathcal{L}$ are computed via the chain rule, and parameters are updated by gradient descent.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Why linear+nonlinear alternation?** Purely linear compositions collapse into a single linear map; the nonlinearity injects expressive power.
- **Why biases?** Biases shift the activation function left/right, decoupling the decision boundary from the origin.
- **Why differentiable activations?** Differentiability is required to compute gradients for parameter updates.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it contains the most-tested equations in Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Neuron Pre-activation | $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}$ (scalar logit) |
| 2 | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Max value $= 0.25$ at $z=0$ |
| 4 | Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(z) = \tfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in [-1,1]$, zero-centered |
| 5 | Tanh Derivative | $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Max value $= 1$ at $z=0$ |
| 6 | ReLU Activation | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Output $\in [0, \infty)$ |
| 7 | ReLU Sub-derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$ | $= 1$ if $z>0$, else $0$ |
| 8 | Leaky ReLU | $\text{LReLU}(z) = \max(\alpha z, z)$, $\alpha \approx 0.01$ | Avoids "dying ReLU" |
| 9 | Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \tfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities sum to **1** |
| 10 | Softmax Derivative | $\tfrac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_j} = \hat{y}_i(\delta_{ij} - \hat{y}_j)$ | Jacobian matrix form |
| 11 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \Vert \hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(n)} - \mathbf{y}^{(n)} \Vert_2^{2}$ | Units: squared error |
| 12 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_n \left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$ | For sigmoid output |
| 13 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | For softmax output (one-hot) |
| 14 | Layer-wise Forward Map | $\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)}(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)})$ | $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} \equiv \mathbf{x}$ |
| 15 | Gradient Descent Update | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 16 | Xavier Initialization | $W^{(\ell)}_{ij} \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[-\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{d_{\ell-1}+d_{\ell}}},\ \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{d_{\ell-1}+d_{\ell}}}\right]$ | For tanh activation |
| 17 | He Initialization | $W^{(\ell)}_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\ \tfrac{2}{d_{\ell-1}}\right)$ | For ReLU activation |
| 18 | Universal Approximation (informal) | A 1-hidden-layer MLP with sufficient hidden units can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary precision. | Theoretical existence, not practical guarantee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Feedforward networks are the workhorses of tabular-data ML (banking, medical risk scoring, recommendation systems), form the *fully-connected head* of CNNs and Transformers, and serve as universal function approximators in reinforcement learning policy networks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition below is written explicitly. No step is skipped — this is the level of rigor expected in KTU Part B (14-mark) derivations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass Through a Single Neuron (Mathematical Form)

Consider a neuron receiving input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, x_3]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ with weights $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, w_3]^{\top}$ and bias $b$.

**Step 1: Compute the pre-activation (dot product).**
The neuron first computes the dot product between the weights and the input:

$$z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b$$

**Step 2: Apply the nonlinear activation.**
Pass the scalar $z$ through the activation function to get the output:

$$h = \sigma(z)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the activation (example: sigmoid).**
Using $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, we obtain:

$$h = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b)}}$$

This is the complete forward-pass equation for a single neuron.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Pass Through an Entire Layer (Matrix Form)

Let layer $\ell$ have $d_{\ell}$ neurons and receive input $\mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\ell-1}}$.

**Step 1: Stack all pre-activations into a vector.**

Each of the $d_{\ell}$ neurons in layer $\ell$ computes its own pre-activation $z_j^{(\ell)}$. Stacking them:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \begin{bmatrix} z_1^{(\ell)} \\ z_2^{(\ell)} \\ \vdots \\ z_{d_\ell}^{(\ell)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{w}_1^{(\ell)\top} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + b_1^{(\ell)} \\ \mathbf{w}_2^{(\ell)\top} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + b_2^{(\ell)} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{w}_{d_\ell}^{(\ell)\top} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + b_{d_\ell}^{(\ell)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Express in compact matrix notation.**
This can be compactly written as:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell \times d_{\ell-1}}$ is the weight matrix and $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell}$ is the bias vector.

**Step 3: Apply the activation element-wise.**

$$\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)} \left( \mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} \right) = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma(z_1^{(\ell)}) \\ \sigma(z_2^{(\ell)}) \\ \vdots \\ \sigma(z_{d_\ell}^{(\ell)}) \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Derivative of the Sigmoid Activation Function

This is the most frequently asked derivative in KTU board exams.

**Step 1: Write the sigmoid function.**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate using the quotient rule or rewrite trick.**
First, rewrite:

$$\sigma(z) = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z})$$

$$= \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify into the canonical form.**
Factor $\sigma(z)$ from the expression:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Notice that $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, and $1 - \sigma(z) = \tfrac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$. Therefore:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This elegant identity is the single most-tested result in deep learning activation-function questions. Memorize it: **the derivative of sigmoid equals sigmoid times one-minus-sigmoid.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ReLU Derivative (Sub-gradient)

**Step 1: Write ReLU.**

$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z) = \begin{cases} z, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate piecewise.**

$$\frac{d}{dz}\text{ReLU}(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

At $z = 0$, the derivative is undefined; we use the sub-gradient $0$ or $1$.

**Step 3: Express compactly using the indicator function.**

$$\text{ReLU}'(z) = \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Softmax + Categorical Cross-Entropy Combined Gradient

This derivation is a KTU favourite for **Part B (14-mark)** questions.

**Step 1: Define the softmax.**
For logits $\mathbf{z} = [z_1, \ldots, z_C]^{\top}$:

$$\hat{y}_i = \text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$$

**Step 2: Define categorical cross-entropy loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$

where $\mathbf{y}$ is one-hot, so $y_c \in \{0,1\}$ with $\sum_c y_c = 1$.

**Step 3: Compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial z_j$.**

We need $\partial \log \hat{y}_c / \partial z_j$, then multiply by $-y_c$ and sum.

First, $\log \hat{y}_c = z_c - \log \sum_k e^{z_k}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \log \hat{y}_c}{\partial z_j} = \delta_{cj} - \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} = \delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the loss derivative.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j} = -\sum_c y_c (\delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j) = -\sum_c y_c \delta_{cj} + \hat{y}_j \sum_c y_c$$

Since $\sum_c y_c = 1$ and the first term collapses to $y_j$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j} = \hat{y}_j - y_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The beautiful result:** the gradient of softmax-cross-entropy w.r.t. logits is simply **prediction minus ground-truth** — $\hat{y}_j - y_j$. This is why this combination is numerically and conceptually clean.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Single-Layer Gradient Update (Worked Numerical Example)

**Problem:** Given a 2-2-1 network (2 inputs, 2 hidden units, 1 output), with inputs $x_1=1$, $x_2=0$, target $y=1$, and sigmoid activations everywhere. Initialize weights $W^{(1)}=[[0.1, 0.2], [0.3, 0.4]]$, $b^{(1)}=[0,0]$, $W^{(2)}=[0.5, 0.6]$, $b^{(2)}=0$. Learning rate $\eta = 0.5$. Compute one gradient-descent update using MSE loss.

**Step 1: Forward pass — hidden layer pre-activations.**

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \cdot 1 + 0.2 \cdot 0 \\ 0.3 \cdot 1 + 0.4 \cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Hidden layer activations.**

$$\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-0.1}} \\ \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-0.3}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5250 \\ 0.5744 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Output pre-activation.**

$$z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = 0.5 \cdot 0.5250 + 0.6 \cdot 0.5744 = 0.2625 + 0.3447 = 0.6072$$

**Step 4: Output prediction.**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.6072) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-0.6072}} = 0.6475$$

**Step 5: Compute MSE loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.6475 - 1)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(-0.3525)^2 = 0.0621$$

**Step 6: Backpropagate to output layer.**
Using MSE derivative $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \hat{y} = (\hat{y}-y)$ and sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = -0.3525 \cdot 0.6475 \cdot (1-0.6475) = -0.3525 \cdot 0.2284 = -0.0805$$

**Step 7: Update output weights.**

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = W^{(2)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = W^{(2)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$$

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = [0.5, 0.6] - 0.5 \cdot (-0.0805) \cdot [0.5250, 0.5744]$$

$$= [0.5, 0.6] + 0.0402 \cdot [0.5250, 0.5744] = [0.5211, 0.6231]$$

**Step 8: Backpropagate to hidden layer** (analogous chain-rule steps omitted for brevity but follow the same pattern).

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, you only need to show 2-3 of these steps clearly; the rest can be summarized in tabular form. Always show the **chain-rule expansion** explicitly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 marks / 3 marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define a deep feedforward network. What is meant by the "depth" of such a network?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A deep feedforward network is a multilayer neural network that maps an input vector $\mathbf{x}$ to an output vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ via a sequence of affine transformations interleaved with nonlinear activation functions, with information flowing strictly in one direction (no feedback loops). Mathematically,

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = f^{(L)} \circ f^{(L-1)} \circ \cdots \circ f^{(1)}(\mathbf{x})$$

The **"depth"** of the network refers to the number of layers $L$ — i.e., the number of successive transformations between input and output. Greater depth enables hierarchical feature learning and exponentially more efficient representations for many functions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Universal Approximation Theorem. Does its existence imply that a single hidden layer is always sufficient in practice?* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** The Universal Approximation Theorem states that a feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to arbitrary precision, provided the activation function is non-constant, bounded, and monotonically increasing (e.g., sigmoid).

**Practical insufficiency:** No. While the theorem guarantees *existence*, it does not guarantee:
- *Learnability* — the required number of hidden units may be impractically large (exponential in input dimension).
- *Generalization* — over-parameterization on a single layer causes severe overfitting.
- *Efficiency* — deep networks achieve the same approximation with exponentially fewer parameters.

Hence, deep architectures are preferred in practice.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Read First!):**
> - **Do NOT skip the chain-rule expansion** — most students lose 3-4 marks by jumping directly to the final gradient. Always write $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \hat{y} \cdot \partial \hat{y}/\partial z \cdot \partial z/\partial W$ explicitly.
> - **Activation derivative must be in canonical form** — e.g., $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ for sigmoid. Writing the quotient-rule form loses a mark.
> - **Confusing depth and width** is a classic 1-mark deduction. *Depth* = number of layers; *Width* = number of neurons per layer.
> - **Softmax derivative:** Always include the Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij}$ — omitting it is a frequent error.
> - **Unit inconsistency:** When working out numerical problems, *always carry 4 decimal places* and clearly state intermediate results. Skipping intermediate steps costs 2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model QP]**

**(a)** Derive the forward propagation equations for a deep feedforward network with $L$ layers, clearly defining all variables. Explain why nonlinear activations are necessary. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-class classification problem using a single hidden-layer network with sigmoid activation, derive the backpropagation gradient $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(2)}$ where $\mathcal{L}$ is the binary cross-entropy loss. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define the architecture.**
Let the input be $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_0}$. For layer $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, L$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)} \left( \mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} \right)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell \times d_{\ell-1}}$ — weight matrix
- $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\ell}$ — bias vector
- $\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{(\ell)}$ — element-wise activation (e.g., sigmoid, ReLU, tanh)

The final prediction is $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{h}^{(L)}$.

**Step 2 — Why nonlinearity is necessary.**
If all activations were identity ($\sigma(z) = z$), the entire network would collapse:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{W}^{(L)} \left( \mathbf{W}^{(L-1)} (\ldots (\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}) + \cdots ) + \mathbf{b}^{(L)} \right)$$

$$= \underbrace{\mathbf{W}^{(L)} \mathbf{W}^{(L-1)} \cdots \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}_{\mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}} \mathbf{x} + \underbrace{\text{(bias terms)}}_{\mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}}}$$

This is a *single linear map*, regardless of depth. A linear network cannot solve non-linearly-separable problems like XOR. Therefore, nonlinear activations are **essential** for universal approximation.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Set up the loss.**
Binary cross-entropy:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$$

where $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$, $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$, and $\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$.

**Step 2 — Chain rule expansion.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial W^{(2)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each factor.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

Multiplying these two:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y} - y$$

**Step 4 — Final factor.**

$$\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$$

Update rule:

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = W^{(2)} - \eta (\hat{y} - y) \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of activation functions in a feedforward network. Compare sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU activations with respect to gradient behaviour and computational efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-class classification problem using softmax output and cross-entropy loss, derive the gradient $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_j$ of the loss with respect to the logit $z_j$ (where $j = 1, 2, 3$). Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Role of activation functions:**
Activation functions introduce **nonlinearity** into the network, enabling it to model complex, non-linear mappings between inputs and outputs. Without them, the network is mathematically equivalent to a single linear transformation regardless of depth.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\tanh(z) = \tfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ |
| Output range | $[0, 1]$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No |
| Gradient saturation | Severe (max $0.25$) | Severe (max $1.0$) but symmetric | None for $z>0$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Very prone | Prone | Avoided for $z>0$ |
| Computation | Expensive (exp) | Expensive (exp) | Cheap (thresholding) |
| Death issue | None | None | "Dying ReLU" ($z \le 0$) |
| Preferred use | Binary output layer | Hidden layers (legacy) | **Default** hidden layer |

**Conclusion:** ReLU is the modern default for hidden layers due to non-saturation and cheap computation; sigmoid is reserved for binary output layers, and softmax for multiclass outputs.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Set up softmax and loss.**

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{3} e^{z_k}}, \quad \mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{3} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$

where $\mathbf{y}$ is one-hot, so exactly one $y_c = 1$ and the others are $0$.

**Step 2 — Express $\log \hat{y}_c$ explicitly.**

$$\log \hat{y}_c = z_c - \log \sum_{k=1}^{3} e^{z_k}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $z_j$.**

$$\frac{\partial \log \hat{y}_c}{\partial z_j} = \delta_{cj} - \frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} \log \sum_k e^{z_k}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} \log \sum_k e^{z_k} = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} = \hat{y}_j$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \log \hat{y}_c}{\partial z_j} = \delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j$$

**Step 4 — Apply chain rule to the loss.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j} = -\sum_{c=1}^{3} y_c (\delta_{cj} - \hat{y}_j) = -\sum_c y_c \delta_{cj} + \hat{y}_j \sum_c y_c$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**
Since $\sum_c y_c = 1$ and the first sum collapses to $y_j$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j} = \hat{y}_j - y_j}$$

This is the elegant, exam-ready result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam Day**

- **Definition (must memorize):** Deep feedforward network = composition of $L$ affine + nonlinear layers mapping $\mathbf{x} \to \hat{\mathbf{y}}$, with no feedback.
- **Depth** = number of layers $L$. **Width** = number of neurons per layer.
- **Forward pass equation:** $\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}\mathbf{h}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)})$.
- **Sigmoid derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ — **memorize this verbatim.**
- **Tanh derivative:** $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$.
- **ReLU derivative:** $\mathbb{1}[z>0]$; Leaky ReLU derivative = $1$ for $z>0$, $\alpha$ for $z \le 0$.
- **Softmax + Cross-entropy gradient:** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_j = \hat{y}_j - y_j$ — clean and elegant.
- **MSE loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum (\hat{y}-y)^2$. **Cross-entropy:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y \log \hat{y}$.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A 1-hidden-layer MLP with sufficient units can approximate any continuous function on a compact set — but depth is more parameter-efficient.
- **Xavier init** for tanh/sigmoid, **He init** for ReLU.
- **Output activation depends on task:** Linear (regression), Sigmoid (binary), Softmax (multiclass).
- **Gradient descent update:** $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mathcal{L}$.
- **Why nonlinearity is essential:** Without it, stacking linear layers collapses into a single linear map — XOR cannot be learned.
- **Architecture design rule-of-thumb (Keras/TensorFlow default):** Start with 1-3 hidden layers, width 2× to 4× input dimension, ReLU activations, He initialization, learning rate $\eta = 10^{-3}$.
- **Numerical care:** Always carry 4 decimal places; explicitly state intermediate gradient values.
- **Common examiner traps:** (i) Skipping chain-rule expansion; (ii) Forgetting $\delta_{ij}$ in softmax; (iii) Confusing depth/width; (iv) Using sigmoid in hidden layers when ReLU is preferable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) - Architecture, Operations, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) - Architecture, Operations, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a specialized class of **deep feed-forward neural networks** that uses a mathematical operation called **convolution** (in place of general matrix multiplication) in at least one of its layers. CNNs are engineered to process data that has a known, grid-like topology — most famously **2D pixel grids (images)**, but also **1D signals** and **3D volumetric data (video/CT scans)**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a CNN is defined as:

> *"A hierarchical, multi-layer neural architecture designed for automatic and adaptive spatial hierarchy learning, composed of three primary building blocks: **Convolutional Layers**, **Pooling Layers**, and **Fully Connected (Dense) Layers**."*

The defining properties that separate a CNN from a plain **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** are:
- **Local Receptive Fields** (each neuron connects only to a small region of the previous layer)
- **Shared Weights** (the same filter is slid across the entire input)
- **Spatial/Temporal Subsampling** (pooling layers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PEPET633 (Deep Learning) Module 2 explicitly requires students to understand the **CNN architecture**, the **mathematical operation of convolution**, **parameter sharing**, **stride/padding**, **pooling**, and **application case studies**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnifying Glass Reading a Photograph"

Imagine you are a detective analyzing a large photograph of a crowded railway station. You don't try to look at the *entire* image at once — that would overwhelm you. Instead, you use a **small magnifying glass** and slide it across the photograph:

1. The **magnifying glass** = a **Convolution Filter (Kernel)** — it focuses on a *small* local patch (e.g., 3×3 pixels).
2. **Sliding the glass across the image** = the **Convolution Operation** — the same lens is reused everywhere, so what you learn about one corner applies to the opposite corner too.
3. **Writing down what you found** in each patch = producing a **Feature Map**.
4. **Stepping back and squinting** so fine details are summarized = a **Pooling Layer**.
5. After scanning the whole image, you have a stack of "feature notes" that an MLP uses to make the final decision ("This is a train station, not an airport").

This mimics how the **human visual cortex (V1 → V2 → V4 → IT)** processes images: simple edges → textures → object parts → whole objects.

## 1.3 Why CNNs Exist — Motivation Over MLPs

For an RGB image of size **H × W × C = 224 × 224 × 3**, the input vector has $224 \times 224 \times 3 = \mathbf{150{,}528}$ dimensions. A single fully-connected layer to a hidden layer of 1024 neurons would require:

$$N_{\text{weights}} = 150{,}528 \times 1024 \approx \mathbf{154 \text{ million parameters}}$$

CNNs solve three critical problems of this approach:

| Problem with MLP | CNN Solution |
|---|---|
| Too many parameters (overfitting) | **Local connectivity** + **weight sharing** |
| Ignores spatial structure of pixels | **Convolution** preserves spatial relationships |
| Sensitive to translation of object | **Pooling** + weight sharing yields **translation equivariance** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Memorize:** A standard $3 \times 3$ filter with $C_{\text{in}} = 64$ channels and $C_{\text{out}} = 64$ channels has only **3,712 parameters** — compared to millions in an equivalent dense layer. This is why CNNs are computationally tractable.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Glossary

- **Filter / Kernel:** A small matrix (e.g., $3 \times 3$) of learnable weights that detects a specific local pattern.
- **Stride ($s$):** The step size (in pixels) the filter moves after each operation.
- **Padding ($p$):** Zero-valued pixels added around the input border to control output spatial size.
- **Feature Map (Activation Map):** The output produced by sliding one filter across the input.
- **Receptive Field:** The region of the original input that ultimately influences a particular neuron.
- **Depth / Channels:** The number of filters applied in a layer (= number of feature maps produced).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CNN Architecture (Hierarchical Pipeline)

A canonical CNN follows this end-to-end pipeline:

$$\text{Input Image} \rightarrow [\text{Conv} \rightarrow \text{ReLU}]^{\times N} \rightarrow \text{Pool} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Flatten} \rightarrow \text{FC} \rightarrow \text{Softmax}$$

### 2.1.1 Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

**A. Convolutional Layer (CONV)**
- Performs the discrete convolution operation.
- Learns a bank of $K$ filters, each of size $f \times f \times C_{\text{in}}$.
- Produces $K$ feature maps stacked along the depth axis.
- Each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a bias and a non-linearity.

**B. Activation Layer (ReLU)**
- Applies element-wise: $a(x) = \max(0, x)$.
- Introduces **non-linearity** without affecting receptive field.
- Variants discussed in advanced topics: Leaky-ReLU, PReLU, GELU.

**C. Pooling Layer (POOL)**
- Performs **spatial downsampling** to:
  1. Reduce spatial dimensions (cheaper computation).
  2. Introduce **local translation invariance**.
- Two types:
  - **Max Pooling:** $y = \max(\text{window})$ — keeps strongest activation.
  - **Average Pooling:** $y = \text{mean}(\text{window})$ — smooths the map.

**D. Fully Connected (FC) Layer**
- Placed near the output; "flattens" the 3D feature volume into a 1D vector.
- Behaves like a standard MLP; used for final classification/regression.

**E. Output Layer**
- **Softmax** for multi-class classification: $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.
- **Sigmoid** for binary classification.
- **Linear** for regression.

## 2.2 The Convolution Operation — The Heart of a CNN

For a 2D input image $I$ and a 2D filter $K$, the discrete convolution is:

$$S(i,j) = (I * K)(i,j) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} I(i+m,\, j+n) \cdot K(m,n)$$

In deep learning literature, this is technically a **cross-correlation** (no kernel flipping), but the term "convolution" is used universally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implementation Trick (im2col):** Modern frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) implement convolution by unrolling each receptive field into a column of a matrix, multiplying it by the flattened filter weights, then reshaping — converting convolution into a single large **GEMM (General Matrix Multiply)**.

## 2.3 Stride and Padding — Controlling Output Dimensions

Let:
- Input size = $W$
- Filter size = $f$
- Stride = $s$
- Padding = $p$

The output spatial dimension is given by:

$$W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

Three canonical padding schemes:
| Padding Type | Value of $p$ | Effect on $W_{\text{out}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Valid** (no padding) | $0$ | $W_{\text{out}} < W$ (shrinks) |
| **Same / Half** | $\dfrac{f-1}{2}$ | $W_{\text{out}} = W$ (preserved for odd $f$) |
| **Full** | $f - 1$ | $W_{\text{out}} > W$ (expands) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these five formulas cover ~80% of CNN numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output spatial size | $W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | All in **pixels** |
| 2 | Parameters per CONV layer | $P_{\text{conv}} = (f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}}) \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$ | Pure number; last term = **bias per filter** |
| 3 | Parameters per FC layer | $P_{\text{fc}} = (n_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot n_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{+1}$ = **bias term** |
| 4 | Memory per layer (activations) | $M = W_{\text{out}} \cdot H_{\text{out}} \cdot C_{\text{out}}$ | **Floats** (×4 bytes for FP32) |
| 5 | Receptive field growth | $r_{l+1} = r_l + (f_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$ | In **pixels** |
| 6 | FLOPs per CONV layer | $\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot C_{\text{out}} \cdot W_{\text{out}} \cdot H_{\text{out}} \cdot (f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}})$ | Multiply–accumulate pair |
| 7 | Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities summing to **1** |
| 8 | Cross-Entropy loss | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | In **nats** (or **bits** with $\log_2$) |

> ⚠️ **Notation Alert:** In the table above, I have used $\prod$ for products and standard parentheses to avoid any pipe `$\vert$` symbols that would break markdown table syntax. Always write $\vert x \vert$ as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, never as `|x|`, when inside tables.

## 2.5 Why These Operations Work — Intuitive Engineering Justification

- **Convolution extracts *local* patterns.** Early layers learn edges and gradients; deeper layers combine these into textures and parts; final layers assemble parts into objects. This mirrors the **Hubel-Wiesel hierarchy** of the visual cortex.
- **Weight sharing exploits the *stationarity* of natural images.** A "horizontal edge detector" is equally useful at the top-left and bottom-right of an image. Sharing weights cuts parameters by orders of magnitude and induces **translation equivariance**.
- **Pooling enforces *locality-invariance*.** A small shift in the cat's position should not change the prediction "cat".
- **Deeper stacks yield exponentially larger receptive fields.** A stack of two $3 \times 3$ convs has an effective receptive field of $5 \times 5$ but with **fewer parameters and more non-linearities** than a single $5 \times 5$ conv — this is the **core design philosophy of VGG-style networks**.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application | CNN Backbone (Example) |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor detection in MRI/CT | U-Net, ResNet-50 |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Lane/pedestrian detection | YOLO, Faster R-CNN |
| **Satellite Imaging** | Crop/forest classification | ResNet, EfficientNet |
| **Industrial QA** | Defect detection on assembly line | Custom small CNNs |
| **NLP** | Sentence classification | 1D-CNN (Kim, 2014) |
| **Speech** | Keyword spotting | 1D-CNN on MFCCs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Dimension Formula

**Given:** Input of size $W$, filter of size $f$, stride $s$, padding $p$.

**Goal:** Derive $W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup.**
Imagine placing the filter's top-left corner at the top-left of the *padded* input. The filter can be placed at horizontal positions $0, s, 2s, 3s, \dots$ until its right edge touches or crosses the right border of the padded input.

**Step 2 — Define the Effective Input Width.**
After adding $p$ zeros on each side, the padded input width is $W + 2p$. The filter occupies $f$ of these positions, so the *sliding* window can travel horizontally by:

$$\text{Total travel} = (W + 2p) - f$$

**Step 3 — Count the Number of Stops.**
If we step by $s$ each time, the number of *additional* stops (beyond the first position) is the floor of the total travel divided by $s$:

$$\text{Number of additional stops} = \left\lfloor \frac{W + 2p - f}{s} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4 — Add the Initial Position.**
Including the very first placement at position $0$, total placements = (additional stops) + 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{out}} &= 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{W + 2p - f}{s} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
&= \left\lfloor \frac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. The floor $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ accounts for the case where the filter cannot fully fit at the edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the floor?** If $(W - f + 2p)$ is not a multiple of $s$, the last partial placement is *dropped* — the formula naturally handles this without an explicit "if" statement.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Output Dimension Calculation

**Problem:** Input image $W = 32$, filter $f = 5$, stride $s = 1$, padding $p = 2$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify numerator.**

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 4}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31 \rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer.**

$$
W_{\text{out}} = 31 + 1 = \mathbf{32}
$$

So the output is the **same size** as the input — this is the **"Same" padding** property for odd $f$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Parameter Count for a CONV Layer

**Problem:** Input volume $224 \times 224 \times 3$. Apply $C_{\text{out}} = 64$ filters of size $f = 3$ with stride $s = 1$, padding $p = 1$.

**Step 1 — Parameter formula.**

$$
P_{\text{conv}} = (f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}}) \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
P_{\text{conv}} = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3) \cdot 64 + 64
$$

**Step 3 — Compute weight term.**

$$
(3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3) = 27, \quad 27 \times 64 = \mathbf{1728}
$$

**Step 4 — Add bias term.**

$$
P_{\text{conv}} = 1728 + 64 = \mathbf{1792 \text{ parameters}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Don't forget the **+1 bias per filter** ($C_{\text{out}}$). Many students lose a mark by writing only $(3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3) \cdot 64 = 1728$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Total Parameters in a Mini CNN

A small CNN has the following layer sequence. Compute **total trainable parameters**.

| Layer | Configuration | Output Size |
|---|---|---|
| Input | $32 \times 32 \times 3$ | $32 \times 32 \times 3$ |
| Conv-1 | $f=5, C_{\text{out}}=6, s=1, p=0$ | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ |
| Pool-1 | $2 \times 2$ max, $s=2$ | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ |
| Conv-2 | $f=5, C_{\text{out}}=16, s=1, p=0$ | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ |
| Pool-2 | $2 \times 2$ max, $s=2$ | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ |
| FC-1 | $400 \to 120$ | $120$ |
| FC-2 | $120 \to 84$ | $84$ |
| FC-3 | $84 \to 10$ | $10$ |

### Step 1 — Parameters in Conv-1.

$$
P_{\text{conv1}} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 3) \cdot 6 + 6 = 450 + 6 = \mathbf{456}
$$

### Step 2 — Parameters in Conv-2.

$$
P_{\text{conv2}} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 6) \cdot 16 + 16 = 2400 + 16 = \mathbf{2416}
$$

### Step 3 — Parameters in Pool layers.

Pooling layers have **zero trainable parameters**. $\Rightarrow P_{\text{pool}} = \mathbf{0}$.

### Step 4 — Parameters in FC-1.

$$
P_{\text{fc1}} = (400 + 1) \cdot 120 = 401 \times 120 = \mathbf{48{,}120}
$$

### Step 5 — Parameters in FC-2.

$$
P_{\text{fc2}} = (120 + 1) \cdot 84 = 121 \times 84 = \mathbf{10{,}164}
$$

### Step 6 — Parameters in FC-3.

$$
P_{\text{fc3}} = (84 + 1) \cdot 10 = 85 \times 10 = \mathbf{850}
$$

### Step 7 — Total.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 456 + 2416 + 0 + 48{,}120 + 10{,}164 + 850 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{62{,}006 \text{ parameters}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $\approx 94\%$ of all parameters are in the FC layers. This is why modern architectures (e.g., **Global Average Pooling in NIN, GoogLeNet**) replace FC layers with lighter alternatives.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Forward Pass Through One Conv Position

Consider a $4 \times 4$ input patch:

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and a $3 \times 3$ filter:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This filter is a **vertical edge detector**. Compute the value at output position $(0, 0)$ with $s = 1, p = 0$.

### Step 1 — Identify the receptive field at $(0,0)$.

It is the top-left $3 \times 3$ sub-matrix of $I$:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the element-wise product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \odot K &= \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 1 & 2\cdot 0 & 3\cdot(-1) \\ 0\cdot 1 & 1\cdot 0 & 2\cdot(-1) \\ 3\cdot 1 & 0\cdot 0 & 1\cdot(-1) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -3 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 \\ 3 & 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum all entries.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(0,0) &= (1 + 0 - 3) + (0 + 0 - 2) + (3 + 0 - 1) \\
&= -2 + (-2) + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation.

A negative response indicates a **vertical intensity gradient** (left side bright → right side dark), confirming this filter activates on **right-to-left vertical edges**.

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Receptive Field Calculation

Three stacked layers: Conv $f=3, s=1$ → Conv $f=3, s=1$ → Conv $f=3, s=2$.

**Compute receptive field after Layer 3.**

### Step 1 — Apply the recursive formula.

$$
r_{l+1} = r_l + (f_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i
$$

### Step 2 — Initialize.

$r_0 = 1$ (a single pixel).

### Step 3 — After Layer 1.

$$
r_1 = 1 + (3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 3
$$

### Step 4 — After Layer 2.

$$
r_2 = 3 + (3 - 1) \cdot s_1 = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 5
$$

### Step 5 — After Layer 3.

$$
r_3 = 5 + (3 - 1) \cdot (s_1 \cdot s_2) = 5 + 2 \cdot (1 \cdot 1) = 7
$$

So the third-layer neuron "sees" a **$7 \times 7$ patch** of the original input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** The stride $s=2$ of the *third* layer does *not* directly affect $r_3$ — only the product of *previous* strides enters. This subtle distinction is a common KTU exam trap.

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Without CNN (MLP) | With CNN |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter count (224×224×3 → 1024) | $\sim 154$ M | $\sim 3$ K to $100$ K |
| Spatial structure | Lost (flattened input) | Preserved (convolution) |
| Translation invariance | Poor | Strong (via pooling + sharing) |
| Inductive bias | None | Locality + stationarity |
| Training data required | Huge | Moderate (with augmentation) |
| Inference speed | Slow | Fast (vectorized GEMM) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CNN Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT IMAGE 32x32x3] --> B[CONV LAYER 1 f=5 C=6]
    B --> C[RELU ACTIVATION]
    C --> D[MAX POOL 2x2 s=2]
    D --> E[CONV LAYER 2 f=5 C=16]
    E --> F[RELU ACTIVATION]
    F --> G[MAX POOL 2x2 s=2]
    G --> H[FLATTEN 400]
    H --> I[FC LAYER 120]
    I --> J[RELU]
    J --> K[FC LAYER 84]
    K --> L[RELU]
    L --> M[FC OUTPUT 10]
    M --> N[SOFTMAX]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style J fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style K fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style L fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```
