# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Q: Define a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). List and briefly explain any three architectural components of a CNN.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a deep feed-forward neural network specifically designed to process data with grid-like topology (such as images) by using the convolution operation in place of general matrix multiplication. It automatically learns spatial hierarchies of features through its layered architecture.

The three main architectural components are:

1. **Convolutional Layer:** Performs the convolution operation using learnable filters (kernels) that slide across the input to extract local features such as edges, textures, and patterns. Each filter shares its weights across the entire input, drastically reducing the parameter count.

2. **Pooling Layer:** Performs spatial downsampling to reduce the size of feature maps. **Max Pooling** selects the maximum value in each window, introducing local translation invariance, while **Average Pooling** computes the average. Pooling reduces computation and controls overfitting.

3. **Fully Connected (FC) Layer:** Placed near the output, it takes the flattened feature vector and performs high-level reasoning. Each neuron is connected to all activations of the previous layer, similar to a traditional MLP, and is responsible for producing the final class scores passed to the Softmax layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark. Three components explained = 1 mark each (concise one-liners are acceptable).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between a Fully Connected (FC) layer and a Convolutional (CONV) layer in terms of connectivity, parameter count, and translation invariance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fully Connected Layer | Convolutional Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Connectivity** | Each neuron connects to *every* activation in the previous layer (global). | Each neuron connects to only a *small local region* (receptive field). |
| **Parameter Count** | Very high ($n_{\text{in}} \times n_{\text{out}}$). | Much lower due to weight sharing ($f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{out}}$). |
| **Weight Sharing** | No sharing — each weight is unique. | Same filter weights are reused across all spatial locations. |
| **Translation Invariance** | Poor — input shifts require relearning. | Strong — weight sharing yields translation equivariance. |
| **Spatial Information** | Lost after flattening. | Preserved throughout the layer. |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the convolution operation, activation function (ReLU), and pooling layer in feature extraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an input image of size $32 \times 32 \times 3$. Apply a convolutional layer with 16 filters of size $5 \times 5$, stride $s = 1$, and padding $p = 2$. Calculate:
  (i) The output feature map size.
  (ii) The number of trainable parameters in this layer.
  (iii) The number of FLOPs (multiply-add operations) for one forward pass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A CNN is a hierarchical deep-learning model designed to automatically learn spatial features from images. Its architecture consists of repeated blocks of **Convolution → ReLU → Pooling**, followed by one or more **Fully Connected** layers and an output activation.

**Block Diagram:**

```
INPUT 32x32x3
    |
[CONV f=5, C=16, p=2] -> ReLU -> [POOL 2x2 s=2]
    |
[CONV f=5, C=32, p=2] -> ReLU -> [POOL 2x2 s=2]
    |
   FLATTEN -> [FC 128] -> ReLU -> [FC 10] -> SOFTMAX
```

**Role of the Convolution Operation:**
The convolution operation extracts **local features** from the input using learnable filters. Each filter slides across the image, computing dot products at every spatial location to produce a 2D **feature map**. Early-layer filters detect low-level features such as edges, corners, and color gradients; deeper filters combine these into textures and object parts. The use of the **same filter across the entire image** (weight sharing) introduces translation equivariance and drastically reduces the parameter count.

**Role of the ReLU Activation:**
ReLU introduces **non-linearity** without affecting the receptive field. It is defined as $f(x) = \max(0, x)$. Without non-linearity, stacking multiple conv layers would collapse into a single linear transformation, making deep networks no more powerful than shallow ones. ReLU also helps mitigate the **vanishing gradient problem** because its derivative is exactly 1 for positive inputs.

**Role of the Pooling Layer:**
Pooling performs **spatial downsampling** by summarizing non-overlapping (or sometimes overlapping) windows. **Max Pooling** retains the strongest activation in each window, providing local translation invariance — small shifts in the input produce the same output. Pooling reduces the spatial size of feature maps by a factor (typically 2), thereby lowering computation, memory, and the number of parameters in subsequent layers. It also acts as a form of **implicit regularization**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $W = 32$, $H = 32$, $C_{\text{in}} = 3$, $C_{\text{out}} = 16$, $f = 5$, $s = 1$, $p = 2$.

**(i) Output Feature Map Size:**

Using $W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$:

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31 \rfloor + 1 = \mathbf{32}
$$

So the output is $32 \times 32 \times 16$.

**(ii) Number of Trainable Parameters:**

Using $P_{\text{conv}} = (f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}}) \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$:

$$
P = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 3) \cdot 16 + 16 = 75 \cdot 16 + 16 = 1200 + 16 = \mathbf{1216}
$$

**(iii) Number of FLOPs (Multiply–Add Operations):**

Each filter produces $32 \times 32 = 1024$ output positions. At each position, the filter computes $5 \times 5 \times 3 = 75$ multiplications and 74 additions. We treat one multiply-add as **2 FLOPs**.

$$
\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot C_{\text{out}} \cdot W_{\text{out}} \cdot H_{\text{out}} \cdot (f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}})
$$

$$
\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 75 = \mathbf{2{,}457{,}600 \text{ FLOPs}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make:
> 1. Forgetting to include the bias term ($+ C_{\text{out}}$) in parameter count → lose 1 mark.
> 2. Using wrong padding substitution → resulting in $W_{\text{out}} = 28$ instead of 32 → lose 2 marks.
> 3. Counting FLOPs as only multiplications (not multiplications + additions) → lose 1 mark.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concepts of **stride**, **padding**, and **parameter sharing** in CNNs. How do they affect the output size and number of parameters? Show with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNN has the following configuration:
- Input: $227 \times 227 \times 3$
- Conv1: $f = 11, s = 4, p = 0, C_{\text{out}} = 96$
- Pool1: $3 \times 3$ max pool, $s = 2$
- Conv2: $f = 5, s = 1, p = 2, C_{\text{out}} = 256$
- Pool2: $3 \times 3$ max pool, $s = 2$

Calculate: (i) Output size after each layer. (ii) Total parameters in both Conv layers. (iii) Memory footprint (in MB) for activations after Conv1 in FP32. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Stride ($s$):**
Stride is the number of pixels the filter shifts at each step. A stride of 1 means the filter moves one pixel at a time, producing dense overlapping outputs. A stride of 2 produces non-overlapping outputs and **halves the spatial dimension**. Stride controls the downsampling rate directly without needing a pooling layer. *Example:* For input $W = 32, f = 3, s = 2, p = 0$, the output is $\lfloor (32 - 3)/2 \rfloor + 1 = 15$.

**Padding ($p$):**
Padding adds zero-valued pixels around the input border before convolution. **Valid padding** ($p = 0$) shrinks the output. **Same padding** ($p = (f-1)/2$ for odd $f$) preserves spatial dimensions. **Full padding** ($p = f - 1$) expands the output. Padding allows deeper networks to keep the spatial resolution stable. *Example:* For input $W = 32, f = 5, s = 1, p = 2$, the output is $\lfloor (32 - 5 + 4)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 32$.

**Parameter Sharing:**
Parameter sharing means the **same filter weights** are used at every spatial location. This exploits the **stationarity** of natural images — an edge detector learned for one corner works everywhere. It reduces parameters by a factor of $W \cdot H$ per filter and provides **translation equivariance** (shifting the input shifts the feature map identically). *Example:* A $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 64$ filter has only $1{,}728$ weights, but is applied $\sim 100{,}000$ times across a $224 \times 224$ input.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Output Sizes:**

**After Conv1** ($W = 227, f = 11, s = 4, p = 0$):

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{227 - 11 + 0}{4} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 54 \rfloor + 1 = 55
$$

Output: $55 \times 55 \times 96$.

**After Pool1** (3×3 max pool, $s = 2$):

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{55 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 26 \rfloor + 1 = 27
$$

Output: $27 \times 27 \times 96$.

**After Conv2** ($W = 27, f = 5, s = 1, p = 2$):

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{27 - 5 + 4}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 27
$$

Output: $27 \times 27 \times 256$.

**After Pool2** (3×3 max pool, $s = 2$):

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{27 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 13
$$

Output: $13 \times 13 \times 256$.

**(ii) Total Parameters (Conv1 + Conv2):**

$$
P_{\text{conv1}} = (11 \cdot 11 \cdot 3) \cdot 96 + 96 = 34{,}848 + 96 = \mathbf{34{,}944}
$$

$$
P_{\text{conv2}} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 96) \cdot 256 + 256 = 614{,}400 + 256 = \mathbf{614{,}656}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 34{,}944 + 614{,}656 = \mathbf{649{,}600}
$$

**(iii) Memory after Conv1 (FP32):**

Each FP32 number occupies 4 bytes.

$$
M = 55 \times 55 \times 96 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}161{,}600 \text{ bytes} \approx \mathbf{1.11 \text{ MB}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in this question:**
> 1. Students often **omit the +1 inside the floor formula** and write $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor (W - f + 2p)/s \rfloor$ — this underestimates by 1.
> 2. Forgetting the bias term in the parameter formula.
> 3. Treating stride > 1 incorrectly — remember the **floor** ensures non-fractional output sizes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU exam revision — memorize these bullets:**

- ✅ **CNN definition:** A deep feed-forward network using **convolution** instead of general matrix multiplication; exploits **local connectivity**, **weight sharing**, and **spatial subsampling**.
- ✅ **Three core building blocks:** **CONV → ReLU → POOL**, repeated, then **FC → Softmax**.
- ✅ **Canonical output-size formula:** $W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - f + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$.
- ✅ **Same padding** requires $p = (f-1)/2$ for odd filter size $f$.
- ✅ **Parameter formula (CONV):** $(f \cdot f \cdot C_{\text{in}}) \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$ — **never forget the bias!**
- ✅ **Parameter formula (FC):** $(n_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot n_{\text{out}}$.
- ✅ **Pooling has zero trainable parameters.**
- ✅ **ReLU:** $a(x) = \max(0, x)$ — derivative is 1 for $x > 0$, 0 otherwise; mitigates vanishing gradients.
- ✅ **Softmax:** $\sigma(z_i) = e^{z_i} \Big/ \sum_j e^{z_j}$ — used in multi-class classification.
- ✅ **Weight sharing** induces **translation equivariance**; **pooling** induces **translation invariance**.
- ✅ **Stacking $3 \times 3$ convs is preferred** over a single $5 \times 5$ conv — fewer parameters, more non-linearities.
- ✅ **Receptive field** grows by $r_{l+1} = r_l + (f_l - 1) \cdot \prod s_i$ — only *previous* strides matter.
- ✅ **Famous CNNs in chronological order:** **LeNet-5 (1998) → AlexNet (2012) → VGG (2014) → GoogLeNet/Inception (2014) → ResNet (2015)**.
- ✅ **Applications:** Image classification, object detection, segmentation, medical imaging, NLP (1D-CNN), speech, satellite imaging, industrial QA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) - Architecture, Operations, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) - Architecture, Operations, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognise patterns in sequences of data, such as text, genomes, handwriting, spoken words, or numerical time-series data sourced from sensors, stock markets, and government agencies. Unlike feedforward neural networks, RNNs exhibit **temporal dynamic behaviour** by maintaining an internal **hidden state (memory)** that is recursively updated at each time step using both the current input and the hidden state from the previous time step.

Mathematically, an RNN is parameterised by three learnable weight matrices — $\mathbf{W}_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden / recurrent), and $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output) — which are **shared across all time steps**, dramatically reducing the number of free parameters compared to a fully unrolled feedforward network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** An RNN is a neural network with **cyclic (recurrent) connections** that allow information to persist across time steps through a hidden state vector $\mathbf{h}_t$, making it suitable for modelling **sequential and temporal dependencies**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Story One Word at a Time"

Imagine you are reading a novel aloud in front of an audience. To understand the **meaning of the current sentence**, you do not just look at the current sentence alone — you carry a **mental summary of all previous sentences** in your working memory. If the story suddenly switches from a cheerful tone to a dark tone, your comprehension of the new sentence is heavily influenced by what you read ten pages ago.

- **The current word** $\rightarrow$ Input $x_t$ at time step $t$
- **Your mental summary of past context** $\rightarrow$ Hidden state $h_t$
- **Your next page of predictions** $\rightarrow$ Output $y_t$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition in One Line:** An RNN is a neural network with a **loop**, allowing information from prior steps to influence the processing of the current step — exactly like how your brain uses prior context to understand new information.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Favourites)

| Term | Symbol | Plain English Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------------|
| Hidden State | $h_t$ | The "memory" of the network at time $t$ |
| Input Vector | $x_t$ | Data fed into the network at time $t$ |
| Output Vector | $y_t$ | Prediction produced at time $t$ |
| Recurrent Weight | $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ | Determines how much past memory to retain |
| Time Step | $t$ | Discrete index of the sequence position |
| Unrolling (Unfolding) | — | Visualising the recurrent loop across $T$ time steps |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the difference between the **folded (compact)** and **unrolled (time-expanded)** representations of an RNN. Memorise both diagrams thoroughly.

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants Referenced

While RNNs are data-driven, the following **standard hyperparameters** are universally cited in the literature and are bold-marked here for emphasis:

- **Hidden state dimensionality ($d_h$):** typically **$\mathbf{64, 128, 256,}$ or $\mathbf{512}$**.
- **Sequence length ($T$):** often between **$\mathbf{20}$ and $\mathbf{500}$** tokens for NLP tasks.
- **Learning rate ($\eta$):** commonly $\mathbf{10^{-3}}$ for Adam optimiser.
- **Gradient clipping threshold:** typically $\mathbf{\Vert g \Vert \leq 5.0}$ to prevent exploding gradients.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Operational Logic

The operational philosophy of an RNN can be broken down into **five sequential logic steps**:

1. **Receive Input at Time $t$**: The network accepts an input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$.
2. **Compute New Hidden State**: The previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ is combined with the current input $x_t$ via a learned affine transformation, followed by a nonlinear activation (typically $\tanh$ or ReLU).
3. **Propagate State Forward**: The newly computed hidden state $h_t$ is passed to the next time step, creating the recurrent feedback loop.
4. **Compute Output**: An output $y_t$ is generated from $h_t$ using a task-specific output transformation (softmax for classification, linear for regression).
5. **Repeat for $T$ Steps**: Steps 1–4 are repeated for each element in the sequence of length $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The parameter sharing across time steps is what makes RNNs **generalise across sequence positions** and remain **statistically efficient** — the same weights are applied regardless of where the pattern appears in the sequence.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value and norm operators use $\vert$ or $\Vert$ (LaTeX) — never the raw pipe symbol `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Equation | Description / Use Case |
|---|---------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hidden State Update | $h_t = \phi_h(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Core recurrence; $\phi_h$ is $\tanh$ or ReLU |
| 2 | Output Computation | $y_t = \phi_y(\mathbf{W}_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ | $\phi_y$ = softmax (classification) or identity (regression) |
| 3 | Tanh Activation | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Squashes hidden state to $[-1, 1]$ |
| 4 | Softmax Activation | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Produces probability distribution over $K$ classes |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_t = - \sum_{i} y_i^{(t)} \log(\hat{y}_i^{(t)})$ | Per-time-step classification loss |
| 6 | Total Sequence Loss | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ | Sum (or mean) of losses over all time steps |
| 7 | BPTT Gradient (Hidden) | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}$ | Backpropagation Through Time chain rule |
| 8 | Gradient Norm (for clipping) | $\Vert g \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i} g_i^2}$ | Used in gradient clipping to prevent explosion |
| 9 | Clipped Gradient | $g \leftarrow \dfrac{g \cdot \text{threshold}}{\Vert g \Vert}$ if $\Vert g \Vert > \text{threshold}$ | Limits gradient magnitude |
| 10 | LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Decides what to discard from cell state |
| 11 | LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Decides what new info to store |
| 12 | LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Decides what to output from cell state |
| 13 | GRU Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | GRU analogue of LSTM's forget + input gates |
| 14 | GRU Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Controls how much past info to forget |
| 15 | BPTT Gradient Bound | $\Vert \dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \Vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh} \Vert^{t-k}$ | Source of vanishing/exploding gradient problem |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Machine translation (Google Translate's earlier seq2seq models), sentiment analysis, named-entity recognition.
- **Speech Recognition:** DeepSpeech (Mozilla), Whisper precursors — converting acoustic signals into text.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather forecasting, energy load forecasting for smart grids.
- **Bioinformatics:** Predicting protein secondary structure, DNA sequence classification.
- **Music Generation:** Composing melodies note-by-note using LSTMs and GRUs.
- **Video Analysis:** Frame-by-frame action recognition, video captioning.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whenever the data has a **temporal or sequential order**, RNNs (or their variants LSTM/GRU) are the **first-choice architecture**, ahead of plain feedforward networks which cannot model order.

## 2.4 Why Standard RNNs Fail — The Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Problem

During **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, the gradient signal must be multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ repeatedly as it travels backward through time. This results in an exponential behaviour:

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

- If the **largest singular value** of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is $> 1$, the product **explodes** $\rightarrow$ unstable training.
- If the **largest singular value** is $< 1$, the product **vanishes** $\rightarrow$ early time steps never get updated.

This is the **canonical motivation** for LSTM and GRU architectures introduced in Section 2 of the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Pass Through a Basic RNN Cell

We derive the step-by-step forward computation for a single RNN cell with **tanh activation** in the hidden layer and **softmax** in the output layer.

**Given:** Input sequence $\mathbf{x} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T\}$, initial hidden state $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 1 — Pre-activation at hidden layer:**

$$a_t^{(h)} = \mathbf{W}_{xh} \, x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

**Step 2 — Apply nonlinear activation (tanh) to produce new hidden state:**

$$h_t = \tanh(a_t^{(h)}) = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh} \, x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

> **Logic:** Tanh squashes the pre-activation into the bounded interval $[-1, 1]$, stabilising the magnitude of $h_t$ and preventing runaway growth of activations.

**Step 3 — Pre-activation at output layer:**

$$a_t^{(y)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy} \, h_t + b_y$$

**Step 4 — Apply softmax to produce probability distribution:**

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(a_t^{(y)}) \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{y}_t^{(i)} = \dfrac{e^{a_t^{(y,i)}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{a_t^{(y,j)}}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute per-step cross-entropy loss against ground truth $y_t$:**

$$L_t = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_t^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(i)}$$

**Step 6 — Aggregate the total sequence loss:**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The total loss is a **sum of per-step losses**, which means BPTT must propagate gradients from **every time step** back to the initial state — a computationally intensive operation for long sequences.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Full Gradient Derivation

The central challenge of BPTT is computing $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$, which depends on $h_t$ for all $t$. We use the **chain rule of calculus** applied across time.

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$.

**Step 1 — Loss depends on $h_t$ directly (via $y_t$) AND indirectly (via $h_{t+1}, h_{t+2}, \ldots, h_T$).** By total derivative:

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the indirect term using the chain rule over time:**

$$\sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \dfrac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{t}} = \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Each Jacobian term is computed via the local derivative of $\tanh$:**

$$\dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\big(1 - \tanh^2(a_j^{(h)})\big) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the product form:**

$$\dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \Big[\text{diag}\big(1 - \tanh^2(a_j^{(h)})\big) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Final gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ using the recursive relation:**

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=t}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \Big[\text{diag}\big(1 - \tanh^2(a_j^{(h)})\big)\Big] \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

> **Logic:** The term $\prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \text{diag}(\cdots) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is what causes the **vanishing/exploding gradient** — it is an **exponential product** of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ and bounded diagonal terms of order $\leq 1$.

**Step 6 — Apply gradient clipping for stability:**

$$\text{If} \quad \Vert g \Vert > \tau \quad \text{then} \quad g \leftarrow g \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{\Vert g \Vert}$$

where $g = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$ and $\tau$ is the **clipping threshold** (typically $\tau = 5$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LSTM Cell Forward Pass

The **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell introduces a **cell state** $C_t$ and three gates to combat vanishing gradients.

**Step 1 — Forget Gate (decides what to throw away from $C_{t-1}$):**

$$f_t = \sigma\big(\mathbf{W}_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\big)$$

**Step 2 — Input Gate (decides what new information to store):**

$$i_t = \sigma\big(\mathbf{W}_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\big)$$

**Step 3 — Candidate Cell State (creates new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$):**

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\big(\mathbf{W}_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\big)$$

**Step 4 — Update Cell State (the core LSTM equation):**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

> **Logic:** The forget gate $f_t$ **multiplicatively** controls memory retention. Crucially, this **additive update** of $C_t$ creates a **gradient highway** that mitigates vanishing gradients — gradients can flow backward through $C_t$ with near-constant magnitude.

**Step 5 — Output Gate (decides what to output from $C_t$):**

$$o_t = \sigma\big(\mathbf{W}_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\big)$$

**Step 6 — Hidden State Output:**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table** — Examiners love asking "Compare RNN, LSTM, and GRU".

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---------|-------------|------|-----|
| Gates | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell State | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Hidden $h_t$ + cell state $C_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| Memory Mechanism | Implicit (vanishing) | Explicit via cell state + gates | Explicit via update gate |
| Parameter Count | Lowest | Highest (~4× vanilla) | Moderate (~3× vanilla) |
| Vanishing Gradient | Severe | Largely solved | Largely solved |
| Training Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Moderate |
| Long Sequences | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| Common Use | Toy examples, short signals | Machine translation, speech | Chatbots, time-series |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Forward Pass for $T=3$

Given: $x_1 = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}, x_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}, x_3 = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$, $h_0 = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{xh} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.3\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hh} = \begin{bmatrix}0.4 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.4\end{bmatrix}$, $b_h = \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix}$.

**Time step $t=1$:**

$$a_1 = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_1 + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_0 + b_h = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.6 \\ 0.3\end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_1 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix}0.6 \\ 0.3\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5370 \\ 0.2913\end{bmatrix}$$

**Time step $t=2$:**

$$a_2 = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_2 + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_1 + b_h = \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.5\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.2148 \\ 0.1165\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.4148 \\ 0.7165\end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_2 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix}0.4148 \\ 0.7165\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3927 \\ 0.6154\end{bmatrix}$$

**Time step $t=3$:**

$$a_3 = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_3 + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_2 + b_h = \begin{bmatrix}0.6 \\ 0.5\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.1571 \\ 0.2462\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.8571 \\ 0.8462\end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_3 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix}0.8571 \\ 0.8462\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.6938 \\ 0.6899\end{bmatrix}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RNN Compact (Folded) Representation

This diagram shows the **folded** view of an RNN — a single cell with a recurrent loop on itself.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["INPUT Xt"] --> nodeM["RNN CELL"]
    nodeM --> nodeY["OUTPUT Yt"]
    nodeM --> nodeH["HIDDEN STATE Ht"]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeM
```

> **Explanation:** The recurrent arrow returning into the same cell represents the **self-loop** that distinguishes RNNs from feedforward networks. The hidden state is fed back into the cell at the next time step.

---

## 4.2 RNN Unrolled (Time-Expanded) Representation

This is the **unrolled** view across $T=3$ time steps — the KTU board's favourite diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1["X1"] --> nodeA1["RNN CELL 1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeY1["Y1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeH1["H1"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeA2["RNN CELL 2"]
    nodeX2["X2"] --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeY2["Y2"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeH2["H2"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeA3["RNN CELL 3"]
    nodeX3["X3"] --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeY3["Y3"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeH3["H3"]
```

> **Explanation:** Each box shares the **same weight matrices** $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$. The horizontal arrows represent the hidden state being passed forward in time — this is the "memory" pathway.

---

## 4.3 LSTM Cell Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The LSTM internal architecture is complex; we render a **functional flow** rather than a physical diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeINP["INPUT Xt"] --> nodeCONCAT["CONCAT Ht-1 AND Xt"]
    nodeH_prev["PREV HIDDEN Ht-1"] --> nodeCONCAT
    nodeCONCAT --> nodeFG["FORGET GATE SIGMA"]
    nodeCONCAT --> nodeIG["INPUT GATE SIGMA"]
    nodeCONCAT --> nodeCG["CANDIDATE TANH"]
    nodeCONCAT --> nodeOG["OUTPUT GATE SIGMA"]
    nodeC_prev["PREV CELL Ct-1"] --> nodeMULT1["MULTIPLY"]
    nodeFG --> nodeMULT1
    nodeIG --> nodeMULT2["MULTIPLY"]
    nodeCG --> nodeMULT2
    nodeMULT1 --> nodeADD["ADD"]
    nodeMULT2 --> nodeADD
    nodeADD --> nodeCt["NEW CELL Ct"]
    nodeCt --> nodeTanhOut["TANH Ct"]
    nodeOG --> nodeMULT3["MULTIPLY"]
    nodeTanhOut --> nodeMULT3
    nodeMULT3 --> nodeH_new["NEW HIDDEN Ht"]
    nodeCt --> nodeH_new
```

> **Explanation:** Three gates (forget, input, output) control the cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ updates. The **additive cell state update** is the key feature that enables long-range memory.

---

## 4.4 Many-to-Many, Many-to-One, One-to-Many RNN Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MANY TO ONE"] --> nodeB["Sentiment Classification"]
    nodeC["ONE TO MANY"] --> nodeD["Music Generation"]
    nodeE["MANY TO MANY"] --> nodeF["Machine Translation"]
    nodeG["SYNC MANY TO MANY"] --> nodeH["Video Frame Labelling"]
```

> **Explanation:** RNNs are classified by their **input-output cardinality**:
> - **Many-to-One:** Sequence $\rightarrow$ single output (e.g., sentiment analysis)
> - **One-to-Many:** Single input $\rightarrow$ sequence (e.g., image captioning)
> - **Many-to-Many (encoder-decoder):** Sequence $\rightarrow$ sequence (e.g., translation)
> - **Synchronous Many-to-Many:** Aligned input-output at each step (e.g., POS tagging)

---

## 4.5 Bidirectional RNN Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1["X1"] --> nodeF1["FORWARD CELL"]
    nodeX2["X2"] --> nodeF2["FORWARD CELL"]
    nodeX3["X3"] --> nodeF3["FORWARD CELL"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3
    nodeX1 --> nodeB3["BACKWARD CELL"]
    nodeX2 --> nodeB2["BACKWARD CELL"]
    nodeX3 --> nodeB1["BACKWARD CELL"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeF3 --> nodeOUT["OUTPUT Yt"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeOUT
```

> **Explanation:** A **Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN)** processes the sequence in **both forward and backward** directions, then concatenates (or sums) the hidden states. This gives the model access to **both past and future context** — useful for tasks like Named Entity Recognition.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Recurrent Neural Network. How does it differ from a feedforward neural network?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a neural network architecture designed for processing sequential data by maintaining a **hidden state** that is updated at each time step using both the current input and the previous hidden state. The recurrence relation is:

$$h_t = f(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

**Differences from feedforward networks:**
1. **Recurrent connections:** RNNs have loops that feed the hidden state back into the network; feedforward networks do not.
2. **Parameter sharing:** RNNs use the **same weights** across all time steps; feedforward networks have separate weights for each layer.
3. **Sequence handling:** RNNs can process **variable-length sequences**; feedforward networks require fixed-size input.
4. **Memory:** RNNs possess temporal memory via $h_t$; feedforward networks are memoryless.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How do LSTMs overcome it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) when gradients are multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ repeatedly across time steps. If the largest singular value of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is less than 1, the gradient magnitude shrinks **exponentially** as it propagates backward, making it impossible for the network to learn **long-range dependencies**.

**LSTM Solution:** LSTMs introduce a **cell state** $C_t$ that is updated via **additive** operations rather than purely multiplicative ones:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

The forget gate $f_t$ controls retention, and because the update is **additive**, gradients can flow backward through time with near-constant magnitude, effectively creating a "gradient highway."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Explain the architecture of a basic RNN with a neat diagram. Derive the forward propagation equations for a single time step. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm with the gradient derivation. Discuss the vanishing and exploding gradient problems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture:** An RNN consists of:
- **Input layer** receiving $x_t$ at each time step.
- **Hidden layer** with recurrent connection carrying $h_{t-1}$.
- **Output layer** producing $y_t$.
- **Shared weights** $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ across all time steps.

**Folded vs Unrolled diagrams:** Refer to diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2 above.

**Forward Propagation Derivation:**

Step 1: Pre-activation at hidden layer:

$$a_t^{(h)} = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$$

Step 2: Hidden state via tanh:

$$h_t = \tanh(a_t^{(h)})$$

Step 3: Pre-activation at output:

$$a_t^{(y)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy} h_t + b_y$$

Step 4: Output via softmax:

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(a_t^{(y)})$$

Step 5: Loss at time $t$:

$$L_t = -\sum_i y_t^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(i)}$$

Step 6: Total loss over sequence:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**BPTT Algorithm:** Backpropagation Through Time unrolls the RNN across all $T$ time steps and applies the standard backpropagation chain rule across the unrolled network.

**Gradient Derivation:**

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=t}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \text{diag}(1-\tanh^2 a_j^{(h)}) \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

**Vanishing Gradient:** When $\|\mathbf{W}_{hh}\| < 1$, the product $\prod \mathbf{W}_{hh}$ shrinks exponentially, causing early-time-step gradients to vanish.

**Exploding Gradient:** When $\|\mathbf{W}_{hh}\| > 1$, the product grows exponentially, causing numerical overflow and unstable training.

**Solutions:** Gradient clipping (for exploding) and LSTM/GRU architectures (for vanishing).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Explain the architecture of LSTM networks. Derive all the gate equations and the cell state update equation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare LSTM and GRU architectures. Explain Bidirectional RNN with a diagram and discuss its applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**LSTM Architecture:** LSTM extends the basic RNN with a **cell state** $C_t$ and **three gates**: forget, input, and output.

**Gate Equations:**

**Forget Gate:**
$$f_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

**Input Gate:**
$$i_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

**Candidate Cell State:**
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Cell State Update (Key Equation):**
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Output Gate:**
$$o_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

**Hidden State Output:**
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

> **Why this works:** The **additive** combination $f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ creates a gradient pathway that does not decay exponentially — gradients flow through the cell state with near-constant magnitude.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table (refer to Section 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix).**

**Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN):** A Bi-RNN consists of two RNNs — one processing the sequence in the **forward** direction and one in the **backward** direction. The final output at time $t$ is computed by combining both hidden states:

$$\overrightarrow{h_t} = \text{RNN}_{fwd}(x_t, \overrightarrow{h_{t-1}})$$
$$\overleftarrow{h_t} = \text{RNN}_{bwd}(x_t, \overleftarrow{h_{t+1}})$$
$$y_t = \mathbf{W}_y [\overrightarrow{h_t}; \overleftarrow{h_t}] + b_y$$

**Advantages:** Access to both past and future context at every time step.

**Applications:**
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER)**
- **Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging**
- **Machine Translation** (encoder side)
- **Protein structure prediction**
- **Handwriting recognition**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting parameter sharing:** RNNs share weights across time steps. Students often write different weights $\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{W}_2, \ldots$ for each cell, which is wrong.
> 2. **Confusing hidden state vs cell state:** In LSTM, $h_t$ (output) and $C_t$ (memory) are different vectors. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Missing the additive update in LSTM:** The cell state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is **additive**, which is why gradients don't vanish. Students often write it as purely multiplicative.
> 4. **No diagram in BPTT question:** Examiners explicitly allocate **2 marks for the unrolled diagram** in BPTT questions. Always draw it.
> 5. **Forgetting the bias term:** Many derivations skip $b_h$ and $b_y$. Include them for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up GRU equations:** GRU uses **update gate $z_t$ and reset gate $r_t$**, NOT forget and input gates like LSTM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **RNNs are for sequential data** — they maintain a hidden state $h_t$ across time steps.
- [x] **Three weight matrices** are shared across time: $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$.
- [x] **Activation functions:** $\tanh$ for hidden state, $\text{softmax}$ for output (classification).
- [x] **BPTT** is backpropagation applied to the unrolled RNN — gradients involve products of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient** occurs when $\|\mathbf{W}_{hh}\| < 1$ — solved by LSTM/GRU.
- [x] **Exploding gradient** occurs when $\|\mathbf{W}_{hh}\| > 1$ — solved by gradient clipping.
- [x] **LSTM has 3 gates:** forget $f_t$, input $i_t$, output $o_t$; plus cell state $C_t$.
- [x] **GRU has 2 gates:** update $z_t$, reset $r_t$; no separate cell state.
- [x] **LSTM cell update is additive:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- [x] **Bidirectional RNNs** combine forward and backward passes for full context.
- [x] **Four RNN topologies:** one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many.
- [x] **Applications:** NLP, speech, time-series forecasting, music generation, bioinformatics.
- [x] **Initial hidden state** $h_0$ is usually zero-initialised or learned.
- [x] **Loss function** for classification = cross-entropy; for regression = MSE.
- [x] **Gradient clipping threshold** typically $\tau = 5$ to prevent explosion.
- [x] **Truncated BPTT** is used for very long sequences to limit the backward pass to $k$ steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loss Functions and Optimization Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Loss Functions and Optimization Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Loss Functions — The "Compass of Error"

A **Loss Function** (also called a *cost function* or *objective function*) is a mathematical scalar metric that quantifies the discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{y}$ produced by a neural network and the ground-truth target $y$. Formally, for a single training example, the loss is denoted as $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$, and for a dataset of $N$ samples, the empirical risk is the average:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\left(y^{(i)}, \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)$$

where $\theta$ represents the complete set of learnable parameters (weights and biases) of the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** A loss function maps a prediction–target pair to a non-negative real number. Lower loss implies a better-fitting model. The choice of loss function is **not universal** — it is dictated by the task (regression vs. classification) and the statistical properties of the output distribution.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bullseye Game 🎯

Imagine you are throwing darts at a dartboard. The **bullseye** is the true value $y$, and your dart's landing point is the prediction $\hat{y}$.

- The **loss function** is the ruler you use to *measure* how far your dart is from the bullseye.
- A *Euclidean ruler* (squared distance) penalizes far-off darts heavily — this is **MSE**.
- A *Manhattan ruler* (absolute distance) treats all errors linearly — this is **MAE**.
- A *custom rubber ruler* that behaves like MSE for small errors and MAE for large errors — this is the **Huber Loss**.

You cannot optimize what you cannot measure. The loss function gives us the **measuring stick**.

---

## 1.2 Optimization Algorithms — The "Blind Mountain Climber"

**Optimization** in deep learning refers to the iterative procedure of updating the model parameters $\theta$ to find the configuration that **minimizes** the loss function $J(\theta)$. Since $J(\theta)$ in modern deep networks is a high-dimensional, non-convex function with millions of parameters, closed-form solutions are infeasible. We rely on **iterative first-order gradient-based methods**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 3 covers classical Gradient Descent (Batch, Mini-Batch, Stochastic), Momentum-based methods (Momentum, Nesterov), Adaptive learning-rate methods (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam), and a conceptual treatment of learning rate schedules.

### Conceptual Analogy — Foggy Mountain Descent 🌫️⛰️

Picture yourself at the top of a mountain in dense fog. You cannot see the bottom, but you can feel the **slope of the ground beneath your feet**.

- The **altitude** at any point is the loss $J(\theta)$.
- The **steepest downward direction** is the negative gradient $-\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$.
- **Gradient Descent** is the act of taking small steps in that steepest direction.
- **Momentum** is like sliding downhill and gaining speed (smoothing zig-zags).
- **Adam** is like a smart hiker who remembers past slopes and adjusts step size per direction (efficient on rocky terrain).

> [!TIP]
> The **learning rate** $\eta$ is your *stride length*. Too small → you never reach the valley. Too large → you overshoot and oscillate. This single hyperparameter is the most critical tuning knob in deep learning.

---

## 1.3 Why Loss + Optimization Form the Heart of Learning

| Component | Role | Key Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Loss Function** | Quantifies error | *"How wrong am I?"* |
| **Optimization** | Updates parameters | *"How do I become less wrong?"* |
| **Together** | Drives learning | *"How does the network actually learn?"* |

Both must be **differentiable** (or sub-differentiable) so that backpropagation can compute gradients with respect to every weight via the chain rule.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Loss Functions

Deep learning losses are categorized by the **nature of the prediction task**.

### 2.1.1 Regression Losses

**(a) Mean Squared Error (MSE) / Quadratic Loss / L2 Loss**
- Used when targets $y$ are continuous real numbers.
- Heavily penalizes outliers due to squaring.
- Always non-negative, differentiable everywhere.

$$L_{\text{MSE}}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The gradient w.r.t. $\hat{y}$ is linear: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \frac{2}{N}(\hat{y} - y)$. This is why MSE paired with linear outputs leads to clean, well-conditioned gradients.

**(b) Mean Absolute Error (MAE) / L1 Loss**
- More robust to outliers than MSE.
- Not differentiable at $y = \hat{y}$ (sub-gradient is used in practice).

$$L_{\text{MAE}}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \right\vert$$

**(c) Huber Loss (Smooth L1)**
- A hybrid: quadratic for small errors, linear for large errors.
- The transition is controlled by the hyperparameter $\delta$.

$$L_{\delta}(y, \hat{y}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^{2} & \text{if } \left\vert y - \hat{y} \right\vert \le \delta \\ \delta \left\vert y - \hat{y} \right\vert - \frac{1}{2}\delta^{2} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.2 Classification Losses

**(d) Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) / Log Loss**
- For binary classification ($y \in \{0, 1\}$, $\hat{y} \in (0, 1)$).
- Drives the output through a sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

$$L_{\text{BCE}}(y, \hat{y}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y^{(i)} \log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The natural pairing in KTU 2024 syllabus: **Sigmoid + BCE** for binary; **Softmax + Categorical Cross-Entropy** for multi-class.

**(e) Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE)**
- For multi-class classification with $C$ mutually exclusive classes.
- Output layer is **softmax**; targets are **one-hot encoded**.

$$L_{\text{CCE}}(y, \hat{y}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{c}^{(i)} \log\hat{y}_{c}^{(i)}$$

**(f) Hinge Loss (SVM-style)**
- Used with class labels $y \in \{-1, +1\}$; encourages a margin.

$$L_{\text{hinge}}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max\left(0, 1 - y^{(i)} \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)$$

### 2.1.3 Distribution-Based Losses

**(g) Kullback–Leibler (KL) Divergence**
- Measures how one probability distribution $P$ diverges from a reference distribution $Q$.

$$D_{\text{KL}}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KL Divergence is **asymmetric**: $D_{\text{KL}}(P \,\Vert\, Q) \neq D_{\text{KL}}(Q \,\Vert\, P)$. It is **not a true metric** (does not satisfy the triangle inequality).

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Optimization Algorithms

### 2.2.1 Vanilla Gradient Descent Variants

| Variant | Batch Size | Update uses | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Full $N$ | Whole dataset per step | Stable, slow per epoch, gets stuck in shallow local minima |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $1$ | One sample per step | Fast updates, very noisy, escapes shallow minima |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $B$ (e.g. 32, 64, 128) | A subset of size $B$ | **Industry standard** — balance of speed and stability |

### 2.2.2 Momentum-Based Methods

**Momentum** adds a fraction $\gamma$ of the previous update to the current gradient, accelerating in consistent directions and damping oscillations.

$$v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - v_{t}$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the momentum coefficient (typically 0.9).

**Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)** "looks ahead" before computing the gradient:

$$v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - v_{t}$$

### 2.2.3 Adaptive Learning Rate Methods

**AdaGrad** — accumulates squared gradients, applies per-parameter scaling:

$$g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t} + \epsilon}} \odot g_{t}$$

where $G_{t} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau}^{2}$ (element-wise) and $\epsilon \approx 10^{-8}$ prevents division by zero.

> [!WARNING]
> AdaGrad's $G_{t}$ **monotonically grows**, causing the effective learning rate to decay to near zero — training stalls.

**RMSProp** — fixes AdaGrad by using an **exponentially decaying moving average** of squared gradients:

$$E[g^{2}]_{t} = \gamma E[g^{2}]_{t-1} + (1 - \gamma) g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^{2}]_{t} + \epsilon}} \odot g_{t}$$

**Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation)** — combines Momentum (first moment) **and** RMSProp (second moment), with bias correction:

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) g_{t}$$

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2}$$

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \qquad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon} \hat{m}_{t}$$

Default hyper-parameters: $\beta_{1} = 0.9$, $\beta_{2} = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$, $\eta = 10^{-3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adam dominates in practice:** Per-parameter adaptive learning rates make it robust to gradient magnitude variance, sparse gradients (NLP), and ill-conditioned loss landscapes. It is the **default optimizer** in modern deep learning frameworks.

### 2.2.4 Learning Rate Schedules

| Schedule | Formula | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Step Decay | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} \cdot \gamma^{\lfloor t/s \rfloor}$ | Drops by factor $\gamma$ every $s$ epochs |
| Exponential Decay | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{0} e^{-kt}$ | Smooth continuous decay |
| Cosine Annealing | $\eta_{t} = \eta_{\min} + \frac{1}{2}(\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min})(1 + \cos(\pi t / T))$ | Smoothly approaches $\eta_{\min}$ |

---

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |

| 1 | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum L(y^{(i)}, \hat{y}^{(i)})$ | Empirical risk | Generic loss |
| 2 | $L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y - \hat{y})^{2}$ | Squared error | Regression |
| 3 | $L_{\text{MAE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \left\vert y - \hat{y} \right\vert$ | Absolute error | Robust regression |
| 4 | $L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum [y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary cross-entropy | Binary classification |
| 5 | $L_{\text{CCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{c} y_{c}\log\hat{y}_{c}$ | Categorical cross-entropy | Multi-class classification |
| 6 | $\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ | GD update rule | All gradient methods |
| 7 | $v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}$ | Momentum update | Momentum |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MSE Loss and Its Gradient

### Problem Setup

Consider a dataset of $N$ samples with targets $y^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}$ and predictions $\hat{y}^{(i)} = f_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$. Define the **empirical Mean Squared Error loss**:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^{2}$$

We need the gradient $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$ for backpropagation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Expand the inner square.** For a single sample $i$, define the per-sample loss:

$$\ell^{(i)} = \left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule.** Differentiate w.r.t. the prediction $\hat{y}^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} = 2 \left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) \cdot (1) = 2\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)$$

The negative sign flips because $\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}}(y - \hat{y})^{2} = 2(y - \hat{y}) \cdot (-1)$, but we are differentiating w.r.t. the model output so the chain continues.

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. parameters $\theta$.** Using the chain rule (the output $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ is a function of $\theta$):

$$\frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta}$$

$$\frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \theta} = 2 \left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 4 — Average over all $N$ samples.** The total loss is the mean, so its gradient is the mean of the per-sample gradients:

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 5 — Vectorize for an entire mini-batch of size $B$ (Batch GD variant).** Let $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{B}$, $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{B}$, and $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times d}$:

$$\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{2}{B}\, \mathbf{X}^{\top} \left(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}\right)$$

This compact form is what frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow compute in a single vectorized backward pass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Binary Cross-Entropy Gradient (with Sigmoid)

### Problem Setup

For a single binary sample, $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ is the sigmoid output, and $y \in \{0, 1\}$.

The BCE loss for one sample is:

$$L = -\left[y \log\hat{y} + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\right]$$

We will derive $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z}$, the gradient w.r.t. the **pre-activation** $z$. This is a *beautiful result* and is the reason sigmoid + BCE is the canonical pairing.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the sigmoid derivative.** A well-known identity:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $L$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left[\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1 - y}{1 - \hat{y}}\right] = -\frac{y(1 - \hat{y}) - \hat{y}(1 - y)}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$y(1 - \hat{y}) - \hat{y}(1 - y) = y - y\hat{y} - \hat{y} + y\hat{y} = y - \hat{y}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply chain rule w.r.t. $z$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \left(-\frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})}\right) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Step 5 — The miraculous cancellation.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = -(y - \hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Elegant Result:** $\boxed{\nabla_{z} L_{\text{BCE}} = \hat{y} - y}$ — the gradient is *linear* in the error, exactly like the MSE gradient with sigmoid outputs. This avoids the **vanishing gradient problem** that occurs when MSE is used with sigmoid, where the gradient is multiplied by $\sigma'(z) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$ and becomes vanishingly small at saturation.

For a mini-batch of $B$ samples, the gradient w.r.t. the pre-activation vector $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{B}$ is:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{z}} L = \frac{1}{B}\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Adam Optimizer Update

### Problem Setup

We have a parameter vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and a stochastic loss function $J(\theta)$ whose gradient $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ is observed at time-step $t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Update the biased first moment estimate $m_{t}$.** This is an exponential moving average of gradients (like Momentum):

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1}) g_{t}$$

**Step 2 — Update the biased second raw moment estimate $v_{t}$.** This is an exponential moving average of squared gradients (like RMSProp):

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2}) g_{t}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bias-corrected estimates.** When $m_{0} = v_{0} = 0$, the estimates are biased toward zero, especially in early steps. The expected value of $m_{t}$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[m_{t}] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} (1 - \beta_{1}) \beta_{1}^{t-\tau} g_{\tau}\right] = (1 - \beta_{1}^{t})\, \mathbb{E}[g_{t}]$$

Hence, the bias-corrected estimator is:

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \qquad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the parameter update.** The update direction is $\hat{m}_{t}$ (smoothed gradient), normalized by $\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}}$ (per-parameter scale):

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon}\, \hat{m}_{t}$$

The $\epsilon$ in the denominator guarantees numerical stability when $\hat{v}_{t}$ is near zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for the KTU exam:** $\hat{m}_{t}/\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}}$ behaves like a *signal-to-noise ratio* per parameter. If a parameter consistently receives large gradients ($\hat{v}_{t}$ is big), its effective learning rate shrinks — preventing divergence. If gradients are rare and small ($\hat{v}_{t}$ is small), the effective learning rate grows — speeding up sparse feature learning.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Optimizer | Memory | Hyper-params | Best For | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Batch GD | $O(d)$ | $\eta$ | Convex small problems | Slow; stuck in local minima |
| SGD | $O(d)$ | $\eta$ | Online learning | Very noisy |
| Mini-Batch GD | $O(d)$ | $\eta, B$ | General | Tuning batch size |
| Momentum | $O(d)$ | $\eta, \gamma$ | Vision CNNs | Overshoots minimum |
| Nesterov | $O(d)$ | $\eta, \gamma$ | RNNs | Slightly higher cost |
| AdaGrad | $O(d)$ | $\eta$ | Sparse data (NLP) | Learning rate decays to 0 |
| RMSProp | $O(d)$ | $\eta, \gamma$ | Non-stationary objectives | Sensitive to $\gamma$ |
| **Adam** | $O(2d)$ | $\eta, \beta_1, \beta_2$ | **Default for most DL** | Can converge to sharp minima |
| AdamW | $O(2d)$ | + weight decay | Transformers | Decoupled WD tuning |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss function. Why is it generally unsuitable for classification tasks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mean Squared Error** loss for a regression task with $N$ samples is defined as:

$$L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^{2}$$

**Why unsuitable for classification:**

1. **Gradient saturation with sigmoid/softmax:** When the final layer is a sigmoid and we use MSE, the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the pre-activation $z$ is proportional to $\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot (\hat{y} - y)$. The factor $\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$ approaches zero at saturation (when $\hat{y} \to 0$ or $\hat{y} \to 1$), causing **vanishing gradients** and stalled learning.
2. **Probabilistic interpretation:** MSE assumes Gaussian noise on the targets, which is a poor assumption for categorical labels. Cross-Entropy is the maximum-likelihood loss for the categorical (Bernoulli / multinomial) distribution.
3. **Non-convexity with sigmoid:** MSE + sigmoid yields a non-convex loss surface, while Cross-Entropy + sigmoid is convex, ensuring better convergence properties.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent. Mention one advantage of Mini-Batch GD over both.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Batch GD | Stochastic GD |
|---|---|---|
| Data used per update | Full dataset ($N$ samples) | One sample at a time |
| Update frequency | Once per epoch | $N$ times per epoch |
| Computational cost per update | High | Low |
| Gradient estimate | Exact, deterministic | Noisy, stochastic |
| Convergence | Smooth, can get stuck in local minima | Oscillates; can escape shallow minima |
| Memory | Needs all data in memory | Stream-friendly |

**Advantage of Mini-Batch GD (1 mark):**

Mini-Batch GD strikes a balance — it uses a batch of $B$ samples (e.g. $B = 32, 64, 128$) per update, giving:
- More stable gradient estimates than pure SGD.
- Faster per-epoch progress than Batch GD.
- Exploits **hardware parallelism** (GPUs are optimized for batched matrix multiplications), leading to significantly higher throughput.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. An internal choice between **OR** options is provided. Below are two complete alternatives.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the **Binary Cross-Entropy loss with a sigmoid output** with respect to the pre-activation $z$, and show why this pairing avoids the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Adam optimizer** in detail. Write its full update equations with bias correction and explain the role of each hyper-parameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Given:** Single binary sample with $y \in \{0, 1\}$, $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, and loss

$$L = -\bigl[y \log\hat{y} + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\bigr]$$

**Step 1 — Sigmoid derivative identity.** We know that

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $L$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left(\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1 - y}{1 - \hat{y}}\right) = -\frac{y(1 - \hat{y}) - (1 - y)\hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$y(1 - \hat{y}) - (1 - y)\hat{y} = y - y\hat{y} - \hat{y} + y\hat{y} = y - \hat{y}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule.** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = -\frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) = -(y - \hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

**Conclusion — Why no vanishing gradient (2 marks):**
The gradient $\hat{y} - y$ is a clean linear function of the prediction error. There is **no multiplicative $\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$ saturation factor**. Even when the sigmoid saturates ($\hat{y} \to 0$ or $\hat{y} \to 1$), the gradient magnitude remains proportional to the error, allowing meaningful updates. This is why sigmoid + BCE is the **mathematically preferred** pairing for binary classification.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation)** is an optimization algorithm that computes *adaptive learning rates* for each parameter by maintaining running estimates of the **first moment** (mean) and the **second raw moment** (uncentered variance) of the gradients.

**Update Equations (4 marks):**

Let $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ be the gradient at time-step $t$.

**1. Update biased first moment estimate:**

$$m_{t} = \beta_{1} m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{1})\, g_{t}$$

**2. Update biased second raw moment estimate:**

$$v_{t} = \beta_{2} v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_{2})\, g_{t}^{2}$$

**3. Compute bias-corrected estimates:**

$$\hat{m}_{t} = \frac{m_{t}}{1 - \beta_{1}^{t}}, \qquad \hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1 - \beta_{2}^{t}}$$

**4. Parameter update:**

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}} + \epsilon}\, \hat{m}_{t}$$

**Role of hyper-parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Default | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ (learning rate) | $10^{-3}$ | Step size scaling the entire update |
| $\beta_{1}$ | $0.9$ | Decay rate for the first moment (controls momentum) |
| $\beta_{2}$ | $0.999$ | Decay rate for the second moment (controls RMSProp-like adaptation) |
| $\epsilon$ | $10^{-8}$ | Numerical stabilizer in the denominator |

**Key Properties:**

- $\hat{m}_{t}$ acts like a smoothed gradient (Momentum-like behavior).
- $\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}}$ normalizes the update per-parameter (RMSProp-like behavior).
- The ratio $\hat{m}_{t}/\sqrt{\hat{v}_{t}}$ is invariant to gradient rescaling → robust to loss landscape scaling.
- Suitable for problems with **sparse gradients** and **non-stationary objectives**.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Momentum** and **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)** optimizers. Compare their update rules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of the AdaGrad optimizer** and explain how **RMSProp** addresses them. Derive the RMSProp update equations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Momentum Optimizer (3.5 marks):**

Momentum accelerates gradient descent by accumulating a velocity vector that is an exponential moving average of past gradients. It dampens oscillations and speeds up convergence in directions of consistent gradient.

**Equations:**

$$v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta\, \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - v_{t}$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the momentum coefficient (typically $\gamma = 0.9$). Intuitively, $v_{t}$ is a "rolling sum" of recent gradients, with geometrically decaying weights.

**Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (3.5 marks):**

NAG modifies momentum by computing the gradient *at the look-ahead position* $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ rather than at the current position. This anticipatory update reduces overshooting near minima.

**Equations:**

$$v_{t} = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta\, \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - v_{t}$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Momentum | Nesterov |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient evaluated at | $\theta_{t-1}$ | $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ (look-ahead) |
| Overshoot near minima | Possible | Reduced |
| Convergence speed | Faster than vanilla GD | Faster than Momentum |
| Use case | General | RNNs, online learning |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Limitations of AdaGrad (3 marks):**

1. **Monotonic accumulation of squared gradients:** $G_{t} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau}^{2}$ grows without bound, causing the effective learning rate $\eta / \sqrt{G_{t} + \epsilon}$ to *decay continuously to zero*.
2. **Training stalls:** Eventually, the learning rate becomes infinitesimally small, and the model stops learning even though the loss may not have converged.
3. **No forgetting mechanism:** AdaGrad cannot "forget" old gradients, even if the loss landscape has changed.

**RMSProp Solution (4 marks):**

RMSProp (Geoff Hinton, 2012) replaces the *monotonic sum* with an **exponentially weighted moving average** of squared gradients, which forgets old gradients and keeps the effective learning rate bounded away from zero.

**Derivation of RMSProp update equations:**

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:**

$$g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$$

**Step 2 — Update the running squared-gradient estimate** with decay rate $\gamma$ (default $0.9$):

$$E[g^{2}]_{t} = \gamma\, E[g^{2}]_{t-1} + (1 - \gamma)\, g_{t}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the parameter update** with per-parameter scaling:

$$\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^{2}]_{t} + \epsilon}}\, g_{t}$$

**Why RMSProp works:**

- The recursive form means $E[g^{2}]_{t}$ is dominated by *recent* gradients, so it adapts to the current curvature.
- Parameters with persistently large gradients get a smaller effective LR; parameters with small gradients get a larger one.
- RMSProp was the precursor to **Adam**, which augments it with first-moment smoothing (momentum).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 3 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias-correction in Adam** — many students write only the biased moment updates and skip $\hat{m}_{t}$ and $\hat{v}_{t}$. KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks specifically for bias correction**. Loss of these marks can drop you below a passing grade in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing $g_{t}^{2}$ with $|g_{t}|$** — in AdaGrad and RMSProp, the second moment is the **element-wise square** of the gradient vector, not its absolute value. Use the symbol $g_{t}^{2}$ (Hadamard product for vectors), not $\lvert g_{t} \rvert$.
> 3. **Mixing up the direction of the gradient sign** — the update is $\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \eta \nabla J$. The negative sign is critical. Writing $+\eta \nabla J$ will lose marks.
> 4. **Using BCE for multi-class problems** — BCE is for binary targets; multi-class requires CCE. Examiners specifically test this discrimination.
> 5. **Not specifying the output activation** — answers that say "use cross-entropy loss" without mentioning *sigmoid* (for BCE) or *softmax* (for CCE) lose a clear 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- 📌 **Loss function** = scalar that quantifies prediction error; must be differentiable (or sub-differentiable).
- 📌 **MSE** suits regression with continuous targets; **BCE/CCE** suit classification.
- 📌 **Sigmoid + BCE** is the canonical binary classification pair; **Softmax + CCE** is the canonical multi-class pair.
- 📌 **Huber loss** combines MSE (for small errors) and MAE (for large errors) — robust to outliers.
- 📌 **KL Divergence** is asymmetric, non-negative, and not a true distance metric.
- 📌 **Gradient Descent** has three flavors — Batch, Stochastic, Mini-Batch. Mini-Batch is the de-facto industry standard.
- 📌 **Momentum** adds a velocity term with decay $\gamma \approx 0.9$ to accelerate descent and dampen oscillations.
- 📌 **Nesterov** evaluates the gradient at the look-ahead position, reducing overshoot.
- 📌 **AdaGrad** adapts LR per parameter via accumulated squared gradients — but suffers from **monotonic decay** to zero.
- 📌 **RMSProp** uses an **exponential moving average** of squared gradients, fixing AdaGrad's decay problem.
- 📌 **Adam = Momentum + RMSProp + Bias Correction**; defaults are $\eta=10^{-3}, \beta_1=0.9, \beta_2=0.999, \epsilon=10^{-8}$.
- 📌 **Learning rate schedules** (step, exponential, cosine annealing) refine the base optimizer for late-stage convergence.
- 📌 For KTU Part-B derivations, **always state the assumptions, show the chain rule, and conclude with a clean boxed result**.
- 📌 The **vanishing gradient problem** with sigmoid + MSE is the *key reason* BCE is preferred over MSE for classification.
- 📌 Adam's bias correction $1 - \beta^t$ ensures unbiased estimates in early training steps when $m_0 = v_0 = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques to Avoid Overfitting - Regularization, Dropout, and Batch Normalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques to Avoid Overfitting - Regularization, Dropout, and Batch Normalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Problem: Overfitting

**Overfitting** is a fundamental challenge in deep learning where a neural network learns the training data *too well*, including its noise, outliers, and random fluctuations. The model memorizes the training set rather than learning the underlying pattern, causing a significant drop in performance on unseen test data. It is characterized by a **large gap between training error and validation error**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** Overfitting occurs when a model has *low training error* but *high generalization error*, typically caused by excessive model capacity, insufficient training data, or excessive training duration.

**Bias-Variance Decomposition:** Total expected error can be expressed as:

$$\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

Overfitting is dominated by the **Variance** component. A high-capacity network has *low bias* but *high variance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Rote-Memorizing Student":**
> Imagine a student preparing for an exam using a single textbook. Instead of understanding the *concepts* and *principles* (generalization), the student memorizes every word, diagram, and page number of that textbook (training set). When the exam paper has slightly different questions (test set), the student fails badly. The three techniques discussed in this module are like:
> - **Regularization** → "The strict teacher who penalizes rote answers and rewards concept-based responses."
> - **Dropout** → "The school that trains students by randomly canceling classes; the student learns independently rather than relying on a single teacher."
> - **Batch Normalization** → "The training coach who ensures all incoming study material is standardized so the student can focus on learning concepts, not on adapting to wildly varying input scales."

### 1.2 Overview of the Three Techniques

| # | Technique | Core Mechanism | Primary Target |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Regularization (L1, L2)** | Adds a penalty on weight magnitude to the loss function | Large weights, model capacity |
| 2 | **Dropout** | Randomly zeroes out neurons during training | Co-adaptation of neurons |
| 3 | **Batch Normalization** | Normalizes layer inputs using mini-batch statistics | Internal Covariate Shift, gradient flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** All three techniques fall under *Module 3 — Optimization and Regularization* and are expected to be examined as both short-answer (3 marks) and full descriptive/derivation (14 marks) questions.

### 1.3 Intuitive Summary

- **Regularization** constrains the *weights* of the network.
- **Dropout** constrains the *network architecture* dynamically during training.
- **Batch Normalization** constrains the *distribution of activations* flowing through the network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Regularization (L1 and L2)

Regularization modifies the standard loss function $J(\mathbf{W}, b)$ by adding a *penalty term* proportional to the magnitude of the weights.

**L2 Regularization (Weight Decay / Ridge):**
$$J_{L2}(\mathbf{W}, b) = J_0(\mathbf{W}, b) + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Vert \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \Vert_F^2$$

where $\Vert \cdot \Vert_F$ is the **Frobenius norm** (sum of squares of all elements in a weight matrix), $\lambda$ is the regularization hyperparameter, and $m$ is the number of training samples.

**L1 Regularization (Lasso):**
$$J_{L1}(\mathbf{W}, b) = J_0(\mathbf{W}, b) + \frac{\lambda}{m} \sum_{l=1}^{L} \sum_{i,j} \vert w_{ij}^{[l]} \vert$$

**Elastic Net (Combined L1 + L2):**
$$J_{\text{EN}}(\mathbf{W}, b) = J_0(\mathbf{W}, b) + \frac{\lambda_1}{m} \sum \vert w \vert + \frac{\lambda_2}{2m} \sum w^2$$

**Gradient Descent Update with L2 Regularization:**

$$w^{[l]} := w^{[l]} - \alpha \left( \frac{\partial J_0}{\partial w^{[l]}} + \frac{\lambda}{m} w^{[l]} \right)$$

$$w^{[l]} := w^{[l]} \left( 1 - \frac{\alpha \lambda}{m} \right) - \alpha \frac{\partial J_0}{\partial w^{[l]}}$$

The factor $\left(1 - \frac{\alpha \lambda}{m}\right)$ is always slightly less than 1, which is why L2 regularization is also called **"weight decay"** — every iteration, weights shrink multiplicatively by this factor before the gradient update.

### 2.2 Dropout

**Dropout** is a stochastic regularization technique proposed by Srivastava et al. (2014). At every training iteration, each neuron (along with its connections) is "dropped" (set to zero) with probability $p$ (the **dropout rate**), independently. The surviving neurons are scaled appropriately to maintain expected activation magnitudes.

**Inverted Dropout Algorithm (Forward Pass for layer $l$):**

**Step 1:** Generate a dropout mask:
$$d^{[l]} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$$

**Step 2:** Apply mask to activations:
$$\tilde{a}^{[l]} = a^{[l]} \odot d^{[l]}$$

**Step 3:** Scale surviving activations (the "inverted" part — done at training time so test time requires no scaling):
$$a^{[l]}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\tilde{a}^{[l]}}{1 - p}$$

At **test time**, dropout is *disabled*, and the full network is used for inference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverted Dropout vs. Standard Dropout:** In *standard dropout*, the scaling $\div(1-p)$ is applied at *test time*. In *inverted dropout* (used in practice, e.g., TensorFlow/PyTorch), the scaling is applied at *training time*, so test-time inference is computationally cleaner.

**Why does Dropout work? (Three accepted justifications):**
1. **Ensemble Effect:** Each forward pass uses a different "thinned" sub-network; the full network is an *ensemble* of $2^n$ such sub-networks (where $n$ is the number of dropout-able units).
2. **Prevents Co-adaptation:** A neuron cannot rely on the presence of specific other neurons, forcing it to learn *more robust, independent* features.
3. **Implicit Regularization:** Dropout approximates an L2 regularizer on the activations, with the regularization strength depending on $p$.

### 2.3 Batch Normalization (BN)

Proposed by Ioffe & Szegedy (2015), BN addresses **Internal Covariate Shift** — the phenomenon where the distribution of layer inputs changes during training as the parameters of previous layers update.

**Forward Pass of Batch Normalization (Training Mode):**

Given a mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}$ of activations for a single neuron:

**Step 1 — Mini-batch Mean:**
$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$$

**Step 2 — Mini-batch Variance:**
$$\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

**Step 3 — Normalize:**
