512 to 1] FP --> OUT[D x scalar probability 0 to 1] ``` > [!NOTE] > The block-level functional architecture above abstracts the DCGAN-style generator–discriminator pipeline. The Generator uses **transposed convolutions with stride 2** for upsampling, while the Discriminator uses **strided convolutions** for downsampling. Batch Normalisation is used in $G$ (but not in $D$'s output layer), and **Leaky ReLU** is preferred in $D$ to prevent the "dying ReLU" problem. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]** ### **Q1.** Define a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). List its two main components and state the role of each. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep generative model that learns to synthesise new samples from a target data distribution through an **adversarial training process** between two neural networks (Goodfellow et al., 2014). The two components are: 1. **Generator (G):** Takes a random noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ as input and produces a synthetic sample $G(z)$ intended to resemble a real data point. Its role is to *fool* the discriminator. 2. **Discriminator (D):** A binary classifier that takes a sample (real or fake) and outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ indicating whether the sample is real. Its role is to *distinguish* real samples from generated ones. Both networks are trained in a **min–max game**: $G$ tries to minimise the discriminator's ability to tell fakes apart, while $D$ tries to maximise its classification accuracy. --- ### **Q2.** What is *mode collapse* in GANs? Mention one technique to mitigate it. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Mode collapse** is a failure mode in GAN training where the Generator learns to produce samples from only a *small subset* of the modes of the true data distribution. For example, when training a GAN on a face dataset containing 50 identities, the Generator might collapse to producing only 2–3 distinct faces, ignoring the remaining 47. This occurs because the Generator exploits a "shortcut" — finding a few samples that consistently fool the Discriminator — and stops exploring the full data manifold. **Mitigation Techniques (any one accepted):** - **Mini-batch discrimination:** Pass *batch-level* statistics to the Discriminator so it can detect when the Generator produces too-similar samples. - **Unrolled GANs:** Update $G$ using gradients computed through *k future steps* of $D$, breaking the myopic shortcut. - **Wasserstein loss with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP):** Provides smoother gradients that discourage mode-seeking behaviour. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme Reference Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **optimal discriminator** $D^{*}(x)$ in a GAN, given a fixed generator $G$. Show that when $p_g = p_{data}$, the discriminator outputs **0.5** for every input. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **min–max training algorithm** of a vanilla GAN, including the alternating update rule. Discuss the practical issue of **vanishing gradients** in the original formulation and explain how the **non-saturating loss** addresses it. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Step 1 — Set up the objective.** For a fixed $G$, the discriminator maximises: $$ V(D) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_{z} p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz $$ **Step 2 — Change of variable.** Let $x = G(z)$ so that $p_z(z) dz$ becomes $p_g(x) dx$: $$ V(D) = \int_{x} \left[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx $$ **Step 3 — Pointwise optimisation.** Differentiate the integrand w.r.t. $D(x)$ and set to zero: $$ \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Solve.** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{data}(x) \cdot (1 - D(x)) &= p_g(x) \cdot D(x) \\ p_{data}(x) &= D(x) \cdot (p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)) \\ D^{*}(x) &= \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Sanity check at equilibrium.** If $p_g = p_{data}$: $$ D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{2 \, p_{data}(x)} = 0.5 \quad \forall \, x $$ The discriminator is reduced to **random guessing** — the defining signature of Nash equilibrium in GANs. ✅ --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **The Min–Max Algorithm (Pseudocode Outline):** For each training iteration: 1. Sample mini-batch of $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z(z)$. 2. Sample mini-batch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{data}$. 3. **Update the Discriminator** by ascending its stochastic gradient: $$ \nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right] $$ 4. **Update the Generator** by descending its stochastic gradient (ascent of $-V$): $$ \nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) $$ In practice, $k$ discriminator updates (typically $k=1$ in DCGAN, $k=5$ in WGAN) are followed by 1 generator update. **The Vanishing Gradient Problem:** In the original (saturating) loss, the generator minimises $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. When the discriminator is strong, $D(G(z)) \to 0$, the gradient $\nabla_G \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ tends to **zero**, and the generator receives essentially no learning signal. **The Non-Saturating Fix:** The generator is instead trained to **maximise** $\log D(G(z))$ (equivalently, minimise $-\log D(G(z))$). Near $D(G(z)) \to 0$: $$ \nabla_{\theta_g} \left[ -\log D(G(z)) \right] = -\frac{\nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z))}{D(G(z))} \to \infty $$ The reciprocal $1/D(G(z))$ **amplifies** the gradient, providing a strong push to the generator. This is the standard practice in PyTorch/TensorFlow GAN implementations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / 2024 Scheme Reference Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN)**. Draw a block-level functional diagram of the generator and discriminator, and discuss three design guidelines proposed by Radford et al. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** formulation. Show that the WGAN loss provides non-vanishing gradients even when the supports of $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ are disjoint. Mention the role of the **1-Lipschitz constraint** and the **gradient penalty (WGAN-GP)**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **DCGAN Architecture:** **Generator:** - **Input:** Latent vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$. - **Layer 1:** Fully-connected layer projects $z$ to a $4 \times 4 \times 1024$ feature map. - **Layers 2–5:** Four **transposed convolution** (fractionally-strided conv) blocks with stride 2, doubling spatial resolution at each step: $4 \times 4 \to 8 \times 8 \to 16 \times 16 \to 32 \times 32 \to 64 \times 64$. - **Output layer:** Tanh activation producing a $64 \times 64 \times 3$ RGB image. **Discriminator:** - **Input:** $64 \times 64 \times 3$ image. - **Layers 1–4:** Four **strided convolution** blocks halving spatial resolution, with **Leaky ReLU** ($\alpha = 0.2$) activations. - **Output layer:** Sigmoid-activated single neuron giving $D(x) \in [0,1]$. **Radford et al.'s Three Key Design Guidelines:** 1. **Replace pooling with strided convolutions:** Use strided convolutions in $D$ and transposed convolutions in $G$ instead of max-pooling or upsampling. This lets the network learn its own spatial downsampling/upsampling. 2. **Use Batch Normalisation in both networks:** Apply BN to all layers except the output of $G$ and the input of $D$, stabilising training by preventing covariate shift. 3. **Use Leaky ReLU in the discriminator:** Avoid the "dying ReLU" problem in $D$ by using Leaky ReLU with slope 0.2, ensuring gradients flow even when activations are negative. $G$ uses standard ReLU. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **The Wasserstein Distance:** WGAN minimises the **Earth-Mover (Wasserstein-1) distance** instead of the JSD: $$ W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_{data}, p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}\left[ \Vert x - y \Vert \right] $$ By the **Kantorovich–Rubinstein duality**, this equals: $$ W(p_{data}, p_g) = \sup_{\Vert f \Vert_L \leq 1} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[f(G(z))] $$ where the supremum is over all **1-Lipschitz functions** $f$. In a neural-network implementation, $f$ is parameterised as $D_w$ (the *critic*), and the WGAN loss is: $$ \mathcal{L}_{critic} = \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[D_w(G(z))] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[D_w(x)] $$ The generator minimises $-W$, equivalent to minimising $\mathcal{L}_{G} = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[D_w(G(z))]$. **Why WGAN avoids vanishing gradients:** When $p_g$ and $p_{data}$ have **disjoint support**, JSD is constant (= $\log 2$) and its gradient is **zero** — original GANs stall. The Wasserstein distance, however, varies *smoothly* with the distribution parameters and provides **gradients everywhere**, even with disjoint supports. The discriminator-as-critic can be trained to optimality, and the magnitude of the loss directly correlates with sample quality — a property absent in vanilla GANs. **The 1-Lipschitz Constraint:** The critic $D_w$ must be 1-Lipschitz: $\Vert D_w(x_1) - D_w(x_2) \Vert \leq \Vert x_1 - x_2 \Vert$ for all $x_1, x_2$. **Weight clipping** was originally used (clamp $w \in [-0.01, 0.01]$), but this caused pathological critic behaviour. **Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP):** Gulrajani et al. (2017) replaced weight clipping with a **soft penalty**: $$ \mathcal{L}_{GP} = \lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\hat{x} \sim \mathbb{P}_{\hat{x}}}\left[ \left( \Vert \nabla_{\hat{x}} D_w(\hat{x}) \Vert_2 - 1 \right)^2 \right] $$ where $\hat{x} = \epsilon x + (1 - \epsilon) G(z)$ is sampled along straight lines between real and fake batches, and $\lambda = 10$ typically. This enforces the Lipschitz constraint smoothly and produces significantly more stable training. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the change of variable $x = G(z)$ in $D^{*}$ derivation:** Marks are lost at Step 2. Always rewrite the second integral in terms of $p_g(x)$ *before* differentiating. > 2. **Confusing the saturating and non-saturating losses:** Many students write $-\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ as the *practical* generator loss. The correct practical loss is $-\log D(G(z))$. > 3. **Stating that WGAN is a "supervised" method:** WGAN is **unsupervised** — the critic is never given class labels; the Lipschitz constraint alone enforces the Wasserstein objective. > 4. **Mixing up IS and FID:** Inception Score (IS) — *higher is better*; Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) — *lower is better*. Examiners explicitly test this. > 5. **Not mentioning Nash equilibrium** when defining GANs: It is the *defining* property of the framework's convergence. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These 15 Bullets Before Entering the Exam Hall** - A GAN consists of a **Generator (G)** and **Discriminator (D)** trained in an adversarial min–max game. - The core objective: $\min_G \max_D \, V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. - The **optimal discriminator** is $D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$, which equals **0.5** at equilibrium. - The **global optimum value** of the value function is $V(G^{*}, D^{*}) = -\log 4$. - The original GAN objective is equivalent (up to a constant) to the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$. - Training alternates: **k updates of D** followed by **1 update of G**. - The **non-saturating loss** $-\log D(G(z))$ fixes the vanishing-gradient issue of the original $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. - **Mode collapse** = Generator produces few modes; mitigated by mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs, or WGAN-GP. - **DCGAN guidelines:** strided convolutions, batch norm, Leaky ReLU in $D$. - **WGAN** uses the Earth-Mover (Wasserstein-1) distance; provides smooth gradients even for disjoint distributions. - **WGAN-GP** uses a gradient penalty $\lambda(\Vert \nabla D \Vert - 1)^2$ to enforce the 1-Lipschitz constraint. - **Conditional GAN (cGAN)** feeds the class label $y$ into both $G$ and $D$ for controllable generation. - **CycleGAN** enables unpaired image-to-image translation using cycle-consistency loss. - **StyleGAN** uses style-based generator and progressive growing for SOTA face synthesis. - Quantitative evaluation metrics: **Inception Score (higher is better)** and **FID (lower is better)**. ---

topics.title: Autoencoders and Their Applications in Anomaly Detection and Compression
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autoencoders and Their Applications in Anomaly Detection and Compression

# Module 4: Autoencoders and Their Applications in Anomaly Detection and Compression

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a specialized class of **unsupervised feedforward neural network** designed to learn a compressed, distributed representation (encoding) of input data and reconstruct the original input from that compressed representation with minimal information loss. Formally, an autoencoder is a neural network trained to copy its input to its output via a **bottleneck architecture** that forces the network to learn a compact latent code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An autoencoder is a self-supervised learning architecture that learns the identity function $f(x) = x$ under constraints that compel the network to discover efficient data encodings in a lower-dimensional latent space. It consists of two coupled sub-networks: an **Encoder** $E: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Z}$ and a **Decoder** $D: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **professional translator who must summarize a 500-page book into just one paragraph, and then expand that one paragraph back into the full book** for a reader who has never seen the original. The translator cannot memorize the book word-for-word (the bottleneck is too tight). Instead, the translator is *forced* to capture only the **essential meaning** — the core themes, characters, and plot — discarding filler details.

- The act of summarization = **Encoding** (high-dimensional input $\mathcal{X} \to$ low-dimensional latent $\mathcal{Z}$)
- The act of expansion = **Decoding** (latent $\mathcal{Z} \to$ reconstructed $\hat{\mathcal{X}}$)
- The bottleneck paragraph = **Latent vector / Code** ($z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d \ll n$)

If the original book and the expanded version are nearly identical, the translator has learned the **essence** of the data. That essence is what we exploit for **anomaly detection** (a fraud case will not reconstruct well because it was never "learned") and **compression** (the latent code is a compressed representation).

### 1.3 Three-Pillar Architecture

| Component | Symbol | Role | Typical Activation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder** | $E_\phi(x) = z$ | Maps input $x$ to latent code $z$ | ReLU, LeakyReLU |
| **Latent (Bottleneck) Layer** | $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Compressed representation | Linear / Tanh |
| **Decoder** | $D_\theta(z) = \hat{x}$ | Reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$ | Sigmoid (for [0,1] data) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always define an autoencoder with the three explicit components — *Encoder, Bottleneck, Decoder*. Examiners award marks specifically for mentioning the **bottleneck constraint** as the reason autoencoders learn useful features rather than trivially copying the input.

### 1.4 The Reconstruction Objective

The autoencoder is trained to minimize a **reconstruction loss** $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$, typically:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \| x_i - \hat{x}_i \|^2$$

For binary inputs (e.g., MNIST binarized to $\{0, 1\}$), **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** is used instead.

### 1.5 Why Autoencoders Cannot Trivially Copy

If the network had unlimited capacity and direct connections $y_i = x_i$, it would simply learn the identity function and learn *nothing useful*. The autoencoder is meaningful **only** because of a **constraint** — the bottleneck has fewer neurons than the input, mathematically $d < n$ where $n = \dim(x)$ and $d = \dim(z)$. This forces the network to **discard irrelevant information** and retain only the most statistically salient features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Undercomplete vs. Overcomplete:**
> - **Undercomplete AE:** $d < n$ (bottleneck smaller than input) — classical use case.
> - **Overcomplete AE:** $d \geq n$ — requires **regularization** (sparsity, denoising, contractive) to prevent trivial identity mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — Layer-by-Layer

Consider a single-hidden-layer autoencoder (deep autoencoders simply stack this logic across multiple layers).

### Encoder Step

Given an input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, the encoder computes a latent representation $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$z = E_\phi(x) = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$$

Where:
- $W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$ is the encoder weight matrix
- $b_e \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the encoder bias vector
- $\sigma_e(\cdot)$ is the encoder activation (commonly **ReLU** or **LeakyReLU**)
- $\phi = \{W_e, b_e\}$ denotes the encoder parameters

### Decoder Step

The decoder reconstructs $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ from the latent code $z$:

$$\hat{x} = D_\theta(z) = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$$

Where:
- $W_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is the decoder weight matrix
- $b_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the decoder bias vector
- $\sigma_d(\cdot)$ is the decoder activation (**sigmoid** for normalized data, **linear** for unbounded)
- $\theta = \{W_d, b_d\}$ denotes the decoder parameters

### Joint Loss Function (MSE Form)

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( x_i - \hat{x}_i \right)^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| x_i - D_\theta(E_\phi(x_i)) \right\|^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mean Squared Error?** MSE penalizes large reconstruction errors quadratically and has a clean gradient structure $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \hat{x} = 2(\hat{x} - x)$, making it suitable for continuous data (images, sensor readings, audio spectrograms). For binary data, BCE is preferred because it operates on probabilities.

## 2.2 Structured Operational Logic

The autoencoder operates in **four logical phases** during each training iteration:

1. **Forward Encoding Pass:** $x \xrightarrow{E_\phi} z$ — compress input to latent code.
2. **Forward Decoding Pass:** $z \xrightarrow{D_\theta} \hat{x}$ — reconstruct from latent code.
3. **Loss Computation:** Compare $x$ and $\hat{x}$ via MSE or BCE to obtain $\mathcal{L}$.
4. **Backpropagation:** Compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_e$, $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_d$ using the **chain rule** and update via gradient descent.

### Encoder Gradient (for a single sample)

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial W_e}$$

### Decoder Gradient

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial W_d}$$

This two-stage chain rule is the **same as standard backpropagation** but applied across the encoder–decoder split.

## 2.3 Undercomplete vs. Overcomplete Autoencoders

| Property | Undercomplete AE | Overcomplete AE |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottleneck dim $d$** | $d < n$ | $d \geq n$ |
| **Capacity constraint** | Architectural (implicit) | Requires regularization (explicit) |
| **Risk of identity learning** | Low | High — would learn $x = \hat{x}$ trivially |
| **Regularization needed?** | No | **Yes** — sparse, denoising, or contractive penalties |
| **Use case** | Compression, anomaly detection | Feature learning, disentanglement |

## 2.4 Regularized Autoencoders (High-Yield for KTU)

### Sparse Autoencoder
Adds an **L1 penalty** on the latent activations to enforce sparsity:

$$\mathcal{L}_{sparse} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d} \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1 - \rho) \log \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \hat{\rho}_j}$$

Where $\rho$ is the desired sparsity target (e.g., 0.05) and $\hat{\rho}_j$ is the empirical mean activation of hidden unit $j$ across a batch. The second term is the **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** between target and actual activation distributions.

### Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)
Trains the network to reconstruct a **clean** $x$ from a **corrupted** version $\tilde{x}$. The loss becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x}|x)} \left[ \| x - D_\theta(E_\phi(\tilde{x})) \|^2 \right]$$

Common corruption: additive Gaussian noise $\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon$, masking noise (random pixels zeroed), or salt-and-pepper noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Denoising Works:** The network cannot simply copy the corrupted input (because it is corrupted) — it must learn the *underlying manifold* of clean data to recover $x$. This forces robust feature learning, similar to dropout but operating on inputs.

### Contractive Autoencoder
Adds a **Jacobian penalty** to penalize sensitivity of the encoder to small input changes:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CAE} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \left( \frac{\partial z_j}{\partial x_i} \right)^2$$

The penalty term $\left\| \nabla_x E_\phi(x) \right\|_F^2$ (Frobenius norm of the encoder Jacobian) forces the latent representation to be **locally contractive** — i.e., robust to small perturbations in the input.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol** | **Meaning / When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| $z = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$ | Encoder mapping | Forward pass compression step |
| $\hat{x} = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$ | Decoder mapping | Forward pass reconstruction step |
| $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | MSE loss | Continuous data reconstruction |
| $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \left[ x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i) \log(1-\hat{x}_i) \right]$ | Binary cross-entropy | Binary / normalized [0,1] data |
| $\text{RE}(x) = \| x - D_\theta(E_\phi(x)) \|^2$ | Reconstruction Error | Anomaly detection score |
| $\text{CR} = \frac{n}{d}$ | Compression Ratio | $d$ = latent dim, $n$ = input dim |
| $\text{BPP} = \frac{d \cdot 32}{N \cdot n}$ | Bits per pixel (image) | Compression quality metric |
| $\mathcal{L}_{sparse} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \beta \cdot D_{KL}(\rho \| \hat{\rho})$ | Sparse AE loss | Regularized sparse feature learning |
| $\mathcal{L}_{CAE} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \lambda \| \nabla_x E_\phi(x) \|_F^2$ | Contractive AE loss | Robustness to perturbations |
| $\mathbb{E}_{q(z\|x)}[\log p(x\|z)] - D_{KL}(q(z\|x) \| p(z))$ | **ELBO** (VAE) | Variational autoencoder objective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lVert x \rVert$ notation in formulas — never the unescaped `|` pipe character in tables, since it breaks markdown rendering.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Why Autoencoder Works** |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing Quality Control | Detect defective products on assembly line | Defects are rare; AE trained on good items flags high RE |
| Cybersecurity | Intrusion detection in network traffic | Normal traffic patterns have low RE; attacks spike RE |
| Medical Imaging | Tumor detection in MRI/CT scans | Pathological regions fail to reconstruct cleanly |
| IoT Sensor Data | Compress sensor streams for low-bandwidth transmission | Latent code transmitted instead of raw high-dim data |
| Image Storage | Dimensionality reduction (alternative to PCA) | Non-linear compression captures non-Gaussian manifolds |
| Recommendation Systems | Collaborative filtering via autoencoder | Reconstruct user-item rating matrix with missing entries |
| Financial Fraud | Anomaly detection in credit card transactions | Fraud patterns are statistically rare and out-of-distribution |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering "applications" questions, always mention *both* the technical mechanism (reconstruction error thresholding) and the *domain-specific reasoning* (why anomalies fail to reconstruct). This dual-level answer scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass Through a 2-Layer Autoencoder

Consider a network with the following structure for an input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

- **Encoder layer 1** (input → hidden1): $h_1 = \sigma(W_1 x + b_1)$, with $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $b_1 \in \mathbb{R}^m$
- **Encoder layer 2 / Bottleneck** (hidden1 → latent): $z = \sigma(W_2 h_1 + b_2)$, with $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$, $b_2 \in \mathbb{R}^d$
- **Decoder layer 1** (latent → hidden1'): $h_1' = \sigma(W_3 z + b_3)$, with $W_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$, $b_3 \in \mathbb{R}^m$
- **Decoder output** (hidden1' → reconstruction): $\hat{x} = \sigma(W_4 h_1' + b_4)$, with $W_4 \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $b_4 \in \mathbb{R}^n$

### Step 1: Encode the Input

The encoder compresses $x$ into the latent code $z$ via two successive affine + nonlinear transformations:

$$h_1 = \sigma(W_1 x + b_1)$$

This step extracts **first-order features** from the input (e.g., edges in images, peaks in spectrograms).

$$z = \sigma(W_2 h_1 + b_2) = \sigma\left( W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 \right)$$

This step produces the **bottleneck code** $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

### Step 2: Decode the Latent Code

The decoder mirrors the encoder:

$$h_1' = \sigma(W_3 z + b_3)$$

$$\hat{x} = \sigma(W_4 h_1' + b_4) = \sigma\left( W_4 \cdot \sigma(W_3 z + b_3) + b_4 \right)$$

### Step 3: Compute the Reconstruction Loss

The total reconstruction error for a single sample:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a common convention to cancel the **2** that appears when differentiating $(x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{x}_k} (x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2 = -2(x_k - \hat{x}_k) = 2(\hat{x}_k - x_k)$$

## 3.2 Full Backpropagation Derivation (Decoder Weight $W_4$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mode:** Every single gradient step is derived explicitly. Do **not** skip the chain rule transitions.

**Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_4}$ where $\hat{x} = \sigma(W_4 h_1' + b_4)$.

**Step 1 — Loss gradient w.r.t. output $\hat{x}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_k} = \hat{x}_k - x_k$$

**Reason:** Direct derivative of $\frac{1}{2}(x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2$ w.r.t. $\hat{x}_k$ gives $(\hat{x}_k - x_k)$.

**Step 2 — Output gradient w.r.t. pre-activation $u_4 = W_4 h_1' + b_4$:**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{x}_k}{\partial u_{4,k}} = \sigma'(u_{4,k})$$

**Step 3 — Pre-activation gradient w.r.t. $W_4$:**

$$\frac{\partial u_{4,k}}{\partial W_4} = h_1'^T$$

**Step 4 — Chain Rule Composition:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_4} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial u_4} \cdot \frac{\partial u_4}{\partial W_4}$$

In matrix form:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_4} = \left( (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma'(u_4) \right) h_1'^T$$

Where $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product.

**Step 5 — Parameter Update (Gradient Descent):**

$$W_4 \leftarrow W_4 - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_4}$$

Where $\eta$ is the learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).

## 3.3 Full Backpropagation Derivation (Encoder Weight $W_1$)

**Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_1}$.

The gradient must flow **backward through three transformations**: output $\to$ $W_4$ $\to$ $W_3$ $\to$ $W_2$ $\to$ $W_1$.

**Step 1 — Delta at output layer:**

$$\delta_{\hat{x}} = (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma'(u_4)$$

**Step 2 — Propagate to $W_3$ gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_3} = \delta_{\hat{x}}^T W_4 \odot \sigma'(u_3) \cdot z^T$$

**Step 3 — Propagate to $W_2$ gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_2} = \left[ W_4^T \delta_{\hat{x}} \odot \sigma'(u_3) \right] \cdot h_1^T$$

**Step 4 — Propagate to $W_1$ gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_1} = \left[ W_2^T \left( W_4^T \delta_{\hat{x}} \odot \sigma'(u_3) \right) \odot \sigma'(u_2) \right] \cdot x^T$$

**Step 5 — Final composition (all layers in one expression):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_1} = \left( W_2^T \left( W_4^T \left[ (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma'(u_4) \right] \odot \sigma'(u_3) \right) \odot \sigma'(u_2) \right) x^T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The gradient of the deepest encoder layer $W_1$ depends on *all subsequent decoder layers*. This is the **credit assignment chain** — early-layer gradients vanish or explode for very deep autoencoders, which is why **residual connections** and **batch normalization** are commonly added.

## 3.4 Derivation: Reconstruction Error as Anomaly Score

The autoencoder is trained on **normal data only** (or normal-dominated data). For a new test point $x^*$:

1. Compute reconstruction: $\hat{x}^* = D_\theta(E_\phi(x^*))$
2. Compute reconstruction error: $\text{RE}(x^*) = \| x^* - \hat{x}^* \|^2$
3. Apply threshold: if $\text{RE}(x^*) > \tau$, classify as anomaly.

**Mathematical Justification:**

For normal data $x \sim p_{\text{normal}}(x)$, the autoencoder has learned a manifold $\mathcal{M}$ approximating the support of $p_{\text{normal}}$. The reconstruction error is the **squared Euclidean distance** from $x^*$ to its projection onto $\mathcal{M}$:

$$\text{RE}(x^*) \approx d^2(x^*, \mathcal{M}) = \min_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \| x^* - D_\theta(z) \|^2$$

**Why anomalies have high RE:** The decoder has never learned to reconstruct out-of-distribution patterns, so it produces a generic "average" reconstruction. The residual $x^* - \hat{x}^*$ is large.

**Threshold Selection (Statistical Approach):**

If reconstruction errors on validation normal data follow distribution $F_{RE}$, set:

$$\tau = \mu_{RE} + k \cdot \sigma_{RE}$$

Where $k \in [2, 3]$ controls sensitivity. Alternatively, use the **95th or 99th percentile** of validation errors.

## 3.5 Derivation: Compression Ratio and Rate-Distortion Trade-off

**Compression Ratio (CR):**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Original Size (bits)}}{\text{Compressed Size (bits)}} = \frac{n \cdot b}{d \cdot b'} = \frac{n}{d} \cdot \frac{b}{b'}$$

Where $b$ is bits per original component (e.g., 8 bits/pixel for standard images) and $b'$ is bits per latent component (e.g., 32 bits for float32).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off:** Higher compression (smaller $d$) → lower CR cost but **higher reconstruction error**. This is the **rate-distortion trade-off**, formally expressed as minimizing the Lagrangian:
> $$\mathcal{L}_{R-D} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\| x - \hat{x} \|^2]}_{\text{Distortion}} + \beta \cdot \underbrace{R(z)}_{\text{Rate (bits)}}$$
> Where $R(z)$ is the entropy of the latent code and $\beta$ balances quality vs. compression.

## 3.6 Derivation: Sparse Autoencoder KL Penalty

The KL divergence between the target Bernoulli distribution $P$ (with parameter $\rho$) and the empirical distribution $Q$ (with mean activation $\hat{\rho}_j$ for unit $j$):

$$D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1 - \rho) \log \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \hat{\rho}_j}$$

**Derivation from KL definition:**

$$D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x \in \{0,1\}} P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$$

$$= \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1 - \rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j}$$

**Empirical mean activation** across a batch of size $N$:

$$\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_j^{(i)}$$

**Sparsity loss contribution for all $d$ latent units:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{sparsity} = \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d} D_{KL}\left( \rho \| \hat{\rho}_j \right)$$

**Final sparse autoencoder objective:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \| x_i - \hat{x}_i \|^2 + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d} D_{KL}\left( \rho \| \hat{\rho}_j \right)$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Autoencoder Variants Comparison

| **Variant** | **Bottleneck Dim** | **Regularizer** | **Primary Use** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla Undercomplete** | $d < n$ | None | Compression | High (basic definition) |
| **Sparse AE** | $d \geq n$ | KL divergence on activations | Feature learning | High (regularization) |
| **Denoising AE** | $d < n$ | Reconstruction from $\tilde{x}$ | Robust features | High (applications) |
| **Contractive AE** | $d < n$ | Jacobian penalty | Robust manifold learning | Medium |
| **Variational AE (VAE)** | $d \ll n$ | KL on latent distribution | Generative modeling | Very High (Module 4) |
| **Convolutional AE** | $d < n$ | None (CNN layers) | Image compression | High (image data) |
| **LSTM-based AE** | $d < n$ | None (RNN layers) | Sequence / time-series | Medium |

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example: Anomaly Detection Threshold

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** Examiners often give a small dataset and ask students to compute reconstruction errors and identify anomalies. Practice this type of problem.

**Given:** Validation set of 5 normal samples yields reconstruction errors: $[0.02, 0.05, 0.03, 0.04, 0.06]$. A new sample has $\text{RE} = 0.18$. Is it anomalous at $k = 2$?

**Step 1 — Compute mean:**

$$\mu_{RE} = \frac{0.02 + 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.04 + 0.06}{5} = \frac{0.20}{5} = 0.04$$

**Step 2 — Compute standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_{RE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} (RE_i - \mu)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \left[ (0.02-0.04)^2 + (0.05-0.04)^2 + (0.03-0.04)^2 + (0.04-0.04)^2 + (0.06-0.04)^2 \right]}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \left[ 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0 + 0.0004 \right]}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.0010}{5}} = \sqrt{0.0002} \approx 0.0141$$

**Step 3 — Compute threshold:**

$$\tau = \mu_{RE} + k \cdot \sigma_{RE} = 0.04 + 2 \cdot 0.0141 = 0.04 + 0.0282 = 0.0682$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$\text{RE}_{test} = 0.18 > \tau = 0.0682 \implies \text{ANOMALY DETECTED}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The test sample's reconstruction error of **0.18** is approximately **2.6 times** the threshold. This strongly indicates the sample does not belong to the normal data distribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autoencoder High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[INPUT X<br/>dim n] --> enc1[ENCODER LAYER 1<br/>ReLU - dim m]
    enc1 --> enc2[ENCODER LAYER 2<br/>ReLU - dim m]
    enc2 --> bottle[BOTTLENECK<br/>LATENT Z<br/>dim d less than n]
    bottle --> dec1[DECODER LAYER 1<br/>ReLU - dim m]
    dec1 --> dec2[DECODER LAYER 2<br/>Sigmoid - dim n]
    dec2 --> outputA[OUTPUT X HAT<br/>RECONSTRUCTION]

    loss1[LOSS FUNCTION<br/>MSE or BCE] -.->|Compare| inputA
    loss1 -.->|Compare| outputA

    bottle --> latentOps[LATENT SPACE<br/>OPERATIONS]
    latentOps --> op1[Compression]
    latentOps --> op2[Anomaly Score]
    latentOps --> op3[Feature Extraction]

    style outputA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900

```

## 4.2 Autoencoder Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Decode X HAT DX]

```

flowchart TD

    B --> C[Compute Validation<br/>Reconstruction Errors]

    D --> E[Deploy Model]

    F --> G[Compute RE Test<br/>Norm X Test minus X HAT squared]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define an autoencoder. What is the role of the bottleneck layer in an autoencoder?**

**Model Answer:**

An **autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network architecture trained to reconstruct its input at the output, where the reconstruction is forced to pass through a **low-dimensional bottleneck** layer (latent code). It consists of two parts: an **encoder** that maps the input $x$ to a compressed latent representation $z = E_\phi(x)$, and a **decoder** that reconstructs $\hat{x} = D_\theta(z)$ from $z$.

**Role of the Bottleneck Layer:**

The bottleneck layer is the **information-constraining element** of the autoencoder. It has fewer neurons than the input dimension ($d < n$), which **prevents trivial identity learning** ($f(x) = x$). This constraint forces the network to learn a **compressed, meaningful representation** of the input — capturing only the most essential features. Without the bottleneck, the autoencoder would simply memorize the input, defeating the purpose of learning useful features. The bottleneck is what enables applications in **dimensionality reduction, compression, and anomaly detection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Scoring Breakdown:** Definition (1 mark), Encoder/Decoder role (1 mark), Bottleneck purpose (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain how an autoencoder can be used for anomaly detection.**

**Model Answer:**

An autoencoder is used for **anomaly detection** by exploiting the **reconstruction error** as an anomaly score. The procedure is:

1. **Training Phase:** The autoencoder is trained on **normal data only**, learning to reconstruct normal patterns with low error.

2. **Testing Phase:** For a new test sample $x^*$, compute the reconstruction $\hat{x}^* = D_\theta(E_\phi(x^*))$ and the reconstruction error $\text{RE}(x^*) = \| x^* - \hat{x}^* \|^2$.

3. **Thresholding:** Compare $\text{RE}(x^*)$ against a threshold $\tau$ (typically set as $\mu_{RE} + k\sigma_{RE}$ on a validation set of normal data).

4. **Decision Rule:**
   - If $\text{RE}(x^*) > \tau$ → **Anomaly detected** (sample does not belong to learned manifold)
   - If $\text{RE}(x^*) \leq \tau$ → **Normal sample**

**Intuition:** The autoencoder has only learned to compress and reconstruct *normal* patterns. When given an *anomalous* input, it produces a poor reconstruction (since the pattern was never learned), yielding a high reconstruction error. This method is widely used in **fraud detection, industrial defect detection, and network intrusion detection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Scoring Breakdown:** Training on normal data (1 mark), Reconstruction error computation (1 mark), Thresholding/decision rule (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of an autoencoder with a neat block diagram. Derive the mathematical formulation of the reconstruction loss using Mean Squared Error (MSE) for a 2-layer encoder and 2-layer decoder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the differences between **vanilla undercomplete autoencoders, sparse autoencoders, and denoising autoencoders**. Include their respective loss functions and use cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part A(a):

**Architecture Overview:**

An autoencoder consists of three main components:

1. **Encoder** $E_\phi$: Compresses input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ into latent $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with $d < n$.
2. **Bottleneck / Latent Layer**: Holds the compressed representation $z$.
3. **Decoder** $D_\theta$: Reconstructs $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ from $z$.

**Block Diagram:**

```
x → [Encoder W1,b1] → h1 → [Encoder W2,b2] → z → [Decoder W3,b3] → h1' → [Decoder W4,b4] → x̂
        ↓                                ↓                ↓                                ↓
       ReLU                             ReLU             ReLU                            Sigmoid
```

**Mathematical Derivation of MSE Loss:**

For a 2-layer encoder, the operations are:

$$h_1 = \sigma(W_1 x + b_1), \quad W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, \quad b_1 \in \mathbb{R}^m$$

$$z = \sigma(W_2 h_1 + b_2), \quad W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}, \quad b_2 \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

For a 2-layer decoder:

$$h_1' = \sigma(W_3 z + b_3), \quad W_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}, \quad b_3 \in \mathbb{R}^m$$

$$\hat{x} = \sigma(W_4 h_1' + b_4), \quad W_4 \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}, \quad b_4 \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**MSE Loss for a single sample:**

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2$$

**For a batch of $N$ samples:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_{i,k} - \hat{x}_{i,k})^2 = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \| x_i - \hat{x}_i \|^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the **2** from squaring differentiation, simplifying the gradient.

---

#### Model Solution for Part A(b):

**Comparison of Three Autoencoder Variants:**

**1. Vanilla Undercomplete Autoencoder:**
- **Bottleneck Constraint:** $d < n$ (architectural compression)
- **Loss Function:** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \| x_i - D_\theta(E_\phi(x_i)) \|^2$
- **Mechanism:** The network learns a low-dimensional projection because the bottleneck is physically smaller than the input.
- **Use Case:** Simple dimensionality reduction, basic compression.
- **Limitation:** Cannot learn rich features if bottleneck is too narrow.

**2. Sparse Autoencoder:**
- **Bottleneck Constraint:** $d \geq n$ (overcomplete)
- **Loss Function:** $\mathcal{L}_{sparse} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d} D_{KL}(\rho \| \hat{\rho}_j)$
- **Mechanism:** The KL divergence penalty forces most latent units to be inactive (near zero), so only a few units fire per input — achieving *sparse activation*.
- **Use Case:** Feature selection, interpretable representations, when input dimension is high but only a few features are informative.
- **Advantage:** Can have $d \geq n$ yet still learn meaningful codes via sparsity.

**3. Denoising Autoencoder:**
- **Bottleneck Constraint:** $d < n$ (typically)
- **Loss Function:** $\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x}|x)} \left[ \| x - D_\theta(E_\phi(\tilde{x})) \|^2 \right]$
- **Mechanism:** Input $x$ is **corrupted** to $\tilde{x}$ (via noise or masking), and the network must reconstruct the *clean* $x$. This forces the model to learn the **data manifold** rather than memorize.
- **Use Case:** Image denoising, robust feature learning, pre-training deep networks.
- **Advantage:** Implicit regularization — the model cannot trivially copy because input is corrupted.

**Summary Table:**

| Property | Vanilla Undercomplete | Sparse AE | Denoising AE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bottleneck | $d < n$ | $d \geq n$ | $d < n$ |
| Regularization | None (architectural) | KL sparsity penalty | Input corruption |
| Trains on | Clean $x$ | Clean $x$ | Corrupted $\tilde{x}$ |
| Best for | Compression | Feature selection | Robustness |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** architecture. How does it differ from a standard autoencoder? Derive the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** objective function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the application of autoencoders in **image compression** and **anomaly detection**. For anomaly detection, show how the threshold $\tau$ is computed using mean and standard deviation of validation reconstruction errors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part B(a):

**Variational Autoencoder (VAE) — Architecture:**

A Variational Autoencoder is a **probabilistic generative model** that learns a **distribution over the latent space** rather than encoding inputs as deterministic points. Instead of mapping $x$ to a single latent vector $z$, the encoder outputs **two vectors**: the mean $\mu(x)$ and the standard deviation $\sigma(x)$ of a Gaussian distribution.

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Encoder:** Outputs $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $\log \sigma^2 \in \mathbb{R}^d$ from input $x$.
2. **Sampling Layer:** Latent code is sampled as $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (reparameterization trick).
3. **Decoder:** Reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from the sampled $z$.

**Differences from Standard Autoencoder:**

| Aspect | Standard AE | Variational AE |
|---|---|---|
| **Latent representation** | Deterministic $z = E(x)$ | Probabilistic $q(z\|x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ |
| **Sampling** | No sampling | Yes — via reparameterization trick |
| **Latent space structure** | Unstructured | Smooth, continuous, regularized |
| **Generation** | Cannot generate new samples | Can sample from $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ and decode |
| **Loss** | MSE only | Reconstruction + KL divergence |

**Derivation of ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound):**

The goal of VAE is to maximize $\log p_\theta(x)$, the log-likelihood of the data. Since $p_\theta(x)$ is intractable, we use the **ELBO** as a tractable lower bound:

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)} \left[ \log p_\theta(x|z) \right] - D_{KL}\left( q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z) \right)$$

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

Starting from the marginal likelihood:

$$p_\theta(x) = \int p_\theta(x, z) \, dz = \int p_\theta(x|z) p(z) \, dz$$

This integral is intractable. Using **Jensen's inequality** and the variational distribution $q_\phi(z|x)$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z|x) \frac{p_\theta(x|z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z|x)} \, dz$$

$$\geq \int q_\phi(z|x) \log \frac{p_\theta(x|z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z|x)} \, dz \quad \text{(Jensen's inequality)}$$

$$= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)} \left[ \log p_\theta(x|z) \right] - D_{KL}\left( q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z) \right)$$

The first term is the **reconstruction term** (likelihood of $x$ given $z$), and the second is the **KL regularization** that keeps $q_\phi(z|x)$ close to the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

**Practical Loss Function (to minimize):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)} \left[ \log p_\theta(x|z) \right] + D_{KL}\left( q_\phi(z|x) \| \mathcal{N}(0, I) \right)$$

**Closed-Form KL Divergence (for diagonal Gaussian $q$ and standard normal $p$):**

$$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \left( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \right)$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part B(b):

**Application 1: Image Compression**

Autoencoders can perform **non-linear image compression** by training on a large image dataset, encoding each image to a compact latent vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., $d = 32$ for an image of $n = 784$ pixels, giving CR = 24.5:1). The latent code is stored/transmitted; the decoder reconstructs the image on demand.

**Pipeline:**

1. Train convolutional autoencoder on training images using MSE loss.
2. Encode each test image: $z = E_\phi(x)$.
3. Store/transmit $z$ (which has fewer numbers than the original image).
4. Decode on receiver: $\hat{x} = D_\theta(z)$.
5. Evaluate quality using **PSNR** (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{255^2}{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{x}_k)^2}$$

Higher PSNR = better reconstruction. A typical good autoencoder achieves **PSNR > 30 dB**.

**Application 2: Anomaly Detection — Threshold Computation**

For anomaly detection, the procedure is:

**Step 1:** Train AE on normal data.
**Step 2:** Compute reconstruction errors on a labeled **validation set of normal samples** (size $M$).
**Step 3:** Compute the **mean** of validation reconstruction errors:

$$\mu_{RE} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{RE}(x_i^{val}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \| x_i^{val} - D_\theta(E_\phi(x_i^{val})) \|^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **standard deviation**:

$$\sigma_{RE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} (\text{RE}(x_i^{val}) - \mu_{RE})^2}$$

**Step 5:** Set the **threshold** at $k$ standard deviations above the mean:

$$\tau = \mu_{RE} + k \cdot \sigma_{RE}$$

Common choice: $k = 2$ (≈95% confidence) or $k = 3$ (≈99.7% confidence).

**Step 6:** For each test sample $x^*$:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Anomaly} & \text{if } \| x^* - D_\theta(E_\phi(x^*)) \|^2 > \tau \\ \text{Normal} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Worked Example (also covered in Section 3.8):** For validation errors $[0.02, 0.05, 0.03, 0.04, 0.06]$, $\mu = 0.04$, $\sigma \approx 0.0141$, threshold at $k=2$ is $\tau = 0.0682$. A test sample with RE = 0.18 is flagged as an **anomaly** (RE $\gg$ $\tau$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Threshold Formula Mistakes:** Students often confuse $\tau = \mu + k\sigma$ with $\tau = \mu \cdot k$ or forget the $k$ multiplier. Always state the **full formula** with the multiplier.
> 2. **Confusing RE for Classification:** Do not say "anomaly if RE = 0" — RE is a *continuous score*, and anomaly is a *thresholded decision*.
> 3. **VAE Derivation:** In Part A(b) of Q-B, students often write ELBO without deriving it via Jensen's inequality. Always show the Jensen step explicitly.
> 4. **Autoencoder ≠ PCA:** Students sometimes claim autoencoders are equivalent to PCA. They are related *only* for linear activations with MSE loss; with non-linear activations, AEs learn **non-linear manifolds**, unlike PCA's linear projections.
> 5. **Confusing Variational AE with Standard AE:** VAEs have a *probabilistic* latent space (mean and variance), not a single deterministic code. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Autoencoders**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ An autoencoder is a self-supervised neural network that learns to reconstruct its input via a **bottleneck** layer.
- ☐ Two components: **Encoder** $E_\phi$ and **Decoder** $D_\theta$.
- ☐ Bottleneck $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with $d < n$ forces compressed feature learning.
- ☐ Loss functions: **MSE** (continuous data), **BCE** (binary data).
- ☐ Training uses standard **backpropagation** with the chain rule across both subnetworks.

**Variants — Must Remember:**
- ☐ **Vanilla Undercomplete AE** — $d < n$, no regularization.
- ☐ **Sparse AE** — KL divergence penalty on latent activations (sparsity target $\rho \approx 0.05$).
- ☐ **Denoising AE** — trains on corrupted input $\tilde{x}$, reconstructs clean $x$.
- ☐ **Contractive AE** — Jacobian penalty $\| \nabla_x E_\phi(x) \|_F^2$ for robustness.
- ☐ **Variational AE (VAE)** — probabilistic latent space; loss = Reconstruction + KL divergence; **ELBO** objective.

**Key Applications:**
- ☐ **Anomaly Detection:** $\text{RE}(x) = \| x - \hat{x} \|^2 > \tau$ where $\tau = \mu_{RE} + k\sigma_{RE}$.
- ☐ **Image Compression:** Store/transmit latent $z$; reconstruct via decoder; quality via PSNR/SSIM.
- ☐ **Dimensionality Reduction:** Non-linear alternative to PCA.
- ☐ **Denoising:** Recover clean $x$ from noisy $\tilde{x}$.
- ☐ **Generative Modeling:** VAE can sample new data from prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$.

**Formulas — High-Yield for KTU:**
- ☐ $z = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$, $\hat{x} = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$
- ☐ $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \| x_i - \hat{x}_i \|^2$
- ☐ $\text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|x)}[\log p_\theta(x|z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z))$
- ☐ $\text{Compression Ratio} = n / d$
- ☐ $\tau = \mu_{RE} + k\sigma_{RE}$ (anomaly threshold)
- ☐ $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(255^2 / \text{MSE})$

**Examiner Mindset:**
- ☐ Always state the **bottleneck constraint** as the key reason autoencoders learn useful features.
- ☐ Always mention **reconstruction error** as the anomaly score.
- ☐ Always distinguish between **deterministic AE** and **probabilistic VAE**.
- ☐ For derivation questions, show **Jensen's inequality** when deriving ELBO.
- ☐ Numericals often ask: compute RE, mean, std, threshold, and classify a test sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, **Part A (3 marks)** questions are short-definition or short-explanation. **Part B (14 marks)** requires depth: architecture, mathematical formulation, variant comparison, and applications. Always pair conceptual statements with formulas to maximize marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of Deep Learning Applications in Various Industries
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Deep Learning Applications in Various Industries

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Case Studies in Deep Learning** refer to documented, real-world deployments of Deep Neural Network (DNN) architectures — including **CNN (Convolutional Neural Networks)**, **RNN/LSTM (Recurrent Neural Networks / Long Short-Term Memory)**, **GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks)**, **Transformers**, and **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** agents — that solve tangible, large-scale business, scientific, or engineering problems across sectors such as Healthcare, Automotive, Finance, Manufacturing, Agriculture, Retail, and Entertainment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a "case study" is **not** a tutorial reproduction. It is a **Problem → Architecture → Dataset → Outcome → Metrics** narrative that demonstrates how theoretical DL constructs deliver measurable industrial value.

### Key Components of an Industrial DL Case Study
- **Problem Statement**: The business/research pain point.
- **Data Modality**: Image, Text, Audio, Time-Series, Tabular, Graph.
- **Architecture Choice**: The DL model family selected and the rationale (e.g., CNN for spatial data, Transformer for sequential/attention-heavy data).
- **Performance Metric**: Domain-specific KPI such as **mAP (mean Average Precision)**, **BLEU score**, **AUROC (Area Under ROC)**, or **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**.
- **Deployment Constraints**: Latency, edge inference, model size, interpretability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Specialist Doctor Network"**
>
> Imagine a multi-specialty hospital. Just as a radiologist interprets X-rays, a cardiologist reads ECGs, and a dermatologist inspects moles — **each industrial DL case study is a "specialist" trained on one organ of the data universe**.
>
> - The **radiologist** ≈ a **CNN** trained on medical scans.
> - The **neurologist** reading EEGs ≈ an **LSTM** trained on time-series sensor streams.
> - The **translator** between two languages ≈ a **Transformer-based Seq2Seq** model.
> - The **forensic sketch artist** reconstructing a face ≈ a **GAN** (e.g., StyleGAN).
>
> The "hospital" is the **industry**, the "doctors" are the DL architectures, and "patients" are the data flowing through them. Deep Learning's industrial power lies in deploying the *right specialist* for the *right data organ*.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Used Across Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourite)**:
> - **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — classification tasks.
> - **mAP (mean Average Precision)** — object detection (e.g., YOLO in autonomous cars).
> - **IoU (Intersection over Union)** — segmentation (e.g., U-Net in tumor delineation).
> - **BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR** — NLP generation.
> - **WER (Word Error Rate)** — speech recognition.
> - **RMSE / MAE** — regression/forecasting.
> - **NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)** — recommender systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework: How to Analyse a DL Case Study (The "5-Layer Lens")

When the KTU board presents an industry case study, examiners expect students to dissect it using this structured lens:

### Layer 1 — Problem Framing
- What business pain-point is being solved?
- Is it **classification, regression, generation, detection, segmentation, translation, or control**?

### Layer 2 — Data Characterization
- **Volume**: Big-data (millions of samples) vs. small-data (few-shot learning).
- **Modality**: Image (CNN/Transformer), Text (RNN/Transformer), Audio (1D-CNN/Wav2Vec), Tabular (MLP/FT-Transformer), Graph (GNN).
- **Labeling**: Supervised, Unsupervised, Self-supervised, Reinforcement.

### Layer 3 — Architecture Selection Rationale
- **CNN** for spatial hierarchies (e.g., tumor detection in MRI).
- **RNN/LSTM/GRU** for temporal sequences (e.g., stock forecasting, ECG).
- **Transformer** for long-range dependencies (e.g., BERT for legal-document QA).
- **GAN** for synthetic-data generation (e.g., data augmentation in pharma).
- **RL (Deep Q-Network, PPO)** for sequential decision-making (e.g., robotics, game-playing agents).

### Layer 4 — Training Strategy
- **Transfer Learning**: Pre-trained backbones (ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT, BERT, GPT).
- **Fine-tuning vs. Feature Extraction**: Decision based on dataset size.
- **Optimization**: Adam, SGD with momentum, learning-rate schedules.
- **Regularization**: Dropout, BatchNorm, L2 weight decay, data augmentation.

### Layer 5 — Industrial Validation Metrics
- Business KPIs: Cost reduction, throughput, customer satisfaction, false-positive cost.
- Technical KPIs: Latency, throughput (inferences/sec), model size (MB).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table condenses every quantitative formula a student may be asked to derive or apply in a case-study question. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $$ \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) $$ | $y_i$: true label, $\hat{y}_i$: predicted probability |
| 2 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $$ \mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 $$ | Used in stock price / demand forecasting |
| 3 | **Precision** | $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ | Critical in medical diagnosis (avoid false alarms) |
| 4 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ | Critical in cancer detection (don't miss positives) |
| 5 | **F1-Score** | $$ F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} $$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |
| 6 | **IoU (Jaccard Index)** | $$ IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} $$ | Tumor segmentation overlap |
| 7 | **mAP (mean Average Precision)** | $$ mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c $$ | Object detection over C classes |
| 8 | **BLEU Score** | $$ BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) $$ | Machine-translation / chatbot evaluation |
| 9 | **Discounted Reward (RL)** | $$ G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k} $$ | Robotics / autonomous-driving policy |
| 10 | **Softmax Output** | $$ \sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} $$ | Multi-class classification head |
| 11 | **Adam Update Rule** | $$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \cdot \frac{\hat{m}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon} $$ | Industry-standard optimizer |
| 12 | **NDCG@k** | $$ NDCG@k = \frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k} $$ | Recommender-system ranking quality |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This analytical framework is the **same one used by AI product managers and ML engineers** to evaluate vendor proposals and research papers. Mastering it equips a KTU graduate for roles in AI consultancy, R&D labs, and intelligent-systems product teams. Every formula above corresponds to a metric reported in published industrial DL deployments — knowing *when* each is appropriate is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this module is **case-study driven** rather than purely theoretical, the "derivation" here is the **logical decomposition of representative industrial cases**. Each step is fully written out with no abbreviations or skip phrases.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Deep Learning Across Industries

### Case Study A — **Healthcare: Diabetic Retinopathy Detection (Google / Aravind Eye Hospital)**

**Step 1 — Problem Identification**
Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of preventable blindness. Manual screening by ophthalmologists is slow and unavailable in rural India. The task: **binary/multi-class classification of retinal fundus photographs** into 5 DR severity grades.

**Step 2 — Data Modality**
- Modality: 2D color fundus images.
- Volume: ~88,000 images (EyePACS dataset, ~40 GB).
- Labeling: Expert-graded by multiple ophthalmologists.

**Step 3 — Architecture Decision**
Inception-v3 (a Google CNN) was chosen because:
- It uses **factorized convolutions** reducing parameters from ~25M to ~24M while increasing depth.
- Pretrained on ImageNet → enables **Transfer Learning**, critical for medical data scarcity.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Forward Pass**
For an input image $x$ of shape $H \times W \times 3$, the convolution operation in the first layer is:

$$
z^{[1]}_{i,j,k} = \sum_{m=0}^{f-1}\sum_{n=0}^{f-1}\sum_{c=0}^{C-1} W^{[1]}_{m,n,c,k} \cdot x_{i+m, j+n, c} + b^{[1]}_k
$$

Where $f$ is filter size (e.g., $3 \times 3$), $C$ is the number of input channels (3 for RGB), and $k$ indexes the $k$-th filter. The activation follows:

$$
a^{[1]}_{i,j,k} = \text{ReLU}(z^{[1]}_{i,j,k}) = \max(0, z^{[1]}_{i,j,k})
$$

**Step 5 — Loss Function Derivation**
For multi-class classification across 5 DR grades, **Categorical Cross-Entropy** is used:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{5} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})
$$

Where $\hat{y}_{i,c}$ is the softmax probability that image $i$ belongs to grade $c$. Minimizing this loss using backpropagation updates the filter weights:

$$
W^{[l]} \leftarrow W^{[l]} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial W^{[l]}}
$$

**Step 6 — Industrial Outcome**
- Achieved **AUROC ≈ 0.99** for referable DR.
- Deployed at Aravind Eye Hospital, screening **> 100,000 patients/year** in India.
- Reduced specialist workload by ~40%.

---

### Case Study B — **Autonomous Driving: Tesla / Waymo Object Detection**

**Step 1 — Problem Identification**
Real-time detection of pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signs from camera and LiDAR streams.

**Step 2 — Data Modality**
- Camera frames (2D RGB) + LiDAR point clouds (3D).
- Volume: Billions of miles of driving data (Tesla fleet).

**Step 3 — Architecture Decision**
**HydraNet** with shared CNN backbone (RegNet/EfficientNet) and multiple task-specific heads (detection, segmentation, depth). For 3D detection, **PointPillars** or **VoxelNet** process LiDAR.

**Step 4 — Loss Function Derivation**
Object detection uses a **multi-task loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{det} = \lambda_{cls}\mathcal{L}_{cls} + \lambda_{box}\mathcal{L}_{box} + \lambda_{obj}\mathcal{L}_{obj}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{L}_{cls}$ = Focal Loss (addresses class imbalance — pedestrians vs. background).
- $\mathcal{L}_{box}$ = Smooth L1 between predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes.
- $\mathcal{L}_{obj}$ = Objectness confidence loss.

$$
\mathcal{L}_{focal} = -\alpha_t (1 - p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)
$$

**Step 5 — Performance Metric**
Reported metric is **mAP@0.5 IoU**. For segmentation tasks:

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} \int_0^1 P_c(R_c) \, dR_c
$$

**Step 6 — Industrial Outcome**
- Real-time inference at **30–60 FPS** on Tesla FSD hardware.
- Disengagement rates (CA DMV reports) reduced by **> 90%** over 5 years.

---

### Case Study C — **Finance: PayPal Fraud Detection**

**Step 1 — Problem Identification**
Classify transactions as fraudulent or legitimate in real-time.

**Step 2 — Data Modality**
- Tabular + sequential transaction history.

**Step 3 — Architecture Decision**
**Deep Wide & Deep model** (Google) combining:
- **Wide linear model** for memorization (memorizing specific fraud rules).
- **Deep feed-forward NN** for generalization (learning hidden fraud patterns).

**Step 4 — Forward Pass**
Wide component:

$$
y_{wide} = W^T x + b
$$

Deep component (stacked layers):

$$
h^{[l+1]} = \text{ReLU}(W^{[l]} h^{[l]} + b^{[l]})
$$

Combined output (sigmoid for binary fraud/no-fraud):

$$
P(\text{fraud} \mid x) = \sigma\left(W_{wide}^T x + W_{deep}^T h^{[\text{last}]} + b\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Industrial Outcome**
- PayPal reports a **~10× improvement** in fraud-detection accuracy over legacy rule-based systems using LSTM + Wide&Deep hybrids.

---

### Case Study D — **NLP: BERT in Legal Contract Analysis**

**Step 1 — Problem**
Extract clauses, classify risk, and answer queries from lengthy legal documents.

**Step 2 — Architecture**
Bidirectional Transformer (BERT-base: 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, ~110M params).

**Step 3 — Self-Attention Derivation**
For input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$:

$$
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
$$

$$
\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Step 4 — Industrial Outcome**
- **ROSS Intelligence** used BERT to reduce legal-research time from hours to minutes.

---

### Case Study E — **Generative AI in Pharma: Insilico Medicine Drug Discovery**

**Step 1 — Problem**
Identify novel molecular structures with desired properties — a $10^{60}$ search space.

**Step 2 — Architecture**
**Conditional GAN / Transformer-based generative model** trained on chemical SMILES strings.

**Step 3 — Industrial Outcome**
- Discovered a novel DDR1 kinase inhibitor in **21 days** (vs. years traditionally).
- Moved to preclinical trials within 18 months.

---

### Case Study F — **Predictive Maintenance in Manufacturing (Siemens / GE)**

**Step 1 — Problem**
Predict equipment failure before it occurs, from vibration, temperature, and acoustic sensors.

**Step 2 — Architecture**
**1D-CNN + LSTM hybrid**: 1D-CNN extracts local vibration features, LSTM models temporal degradation.

**Step 3 — Industrial Outcome**
- Siemens Amberg factory reports **> 99% predictive accuracy** and **25% reduction** in maintenance costs.

---

### Case Study G — **Precision Agriculture (Blue River Technology / John Deere)**

**Step 1 — Problem**
Identify weeds vs. crops in real-time from tractor-mounted cameras and spray herbicide selectively.

**Step 2 — Architecture**
**SegNet / U-Net** for pixel-level semantic segmentation.

**Step 3 — Industrial Outcome**
- **See & Spray** technology reduces herbicide usage by **~77%**, saving farmers millions.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
**List any three real-world industrial applications of Deep Learning and identify the deep learning architecture best suited for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| # | Industry Application | Best-Suited Architecture | Reason |
|---|----------------------|---------------------------|--------|
| 1 | **Tumor segmentation in MRI scans** | **U-Net (CNN variant)** | U-Net's encoder–decoder with skip connections excels at pixel-wise biomedical image segmentation, preserving spatial detail. |
| 2 | **Stock price forecasting** | **LSTM / GRU (RNN variants)** | They capture long-term temporal dependencies in sequential financial time-series, mitigating the vanishing-gradient problem of vanilla RNNs. |
| 3 | **Autonomous vehicle perception (object detection)** | **CNN-based detectors (YOLO, Faster R-CNN)** | They extract hierarchical spatial features from camera frames and output bounding boxes in real time (>30 FPS). |

*(1 mark per correct pairing with valid reasoning.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
**Differentiate between Transfer Learning and Training a CNN from scratch. Why is Transfer Learning preferred in most industrial medical-imaging case studies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Training from scratch**: All convolutional filter weights are initialized randomly and learned using backpropagation on the target dataset. Requires **millions of labelled images** and massive compute.
- **Transfer Learning**: A CNN pre-trained on a large source dataset (e.g., ImageNet with 1.4 M images / 1000 classes) is reused as a feature extractor or fine-tuned on a smaller target dataset (e.g., 10 000 X-ray images).
- **Why preferred in medical imaging**:
  1. Labelled medical data is **scarce and expensive** (expert annotation).
  2. Early CNN layers learn **universal features** (edges, textures) transferable across domains.
  3. Reduces **training time** from days to hours and improves **generalization** with limited data.

*(2 marks for the difference + 1 mark for medical-imaging justification.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain with a real-world case study how **CNNs are deployed in medical image diagnosis**. Cover: (i) problem statement, (ii) dataset, (iii) architecture, (iv) loss function, (v) performance metric, (vi) industrial impact.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the role of **Transformer architectures (BERT/GPT)** in industrial NLP applications. Compare with traditional RNN-based models, citing at least two industrial case studies.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark)**
Skin-cancer (melanoma) detection from dermoscopic images is a critical diagnostic task. Manual diagnosis by dermatologists is time-consuming and error-prone.

**Step 2 — Dataset (1 mark)**
**ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)** dataset — ~70 000 dermoscopic images labelled as benign or malignant across 8 categories.

**Step 3 — Architecture (2 marks)**
**EfficientNet-B4** (a CNN) is selected because of its compound-scaling method that uniformly scales depth, width, and resolution. It uses:
- **MBConv (Mobile Inverted Bottleneck)** blocks with **Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE)** attention.
- A **global average pooling** layer feeding a softmax classifier.
Transfer learning from ImageNet weights is applied; final layer replaced with a 2-class softmax for binary classification (benign/malignant).

**Step 4 — Loss Function (1 mark)**
Since the dataset is **class-imbalanced** (more benign than malignant samples), **Focal Loss** is used:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{focal} = -\alpha_t (1 - p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)
$$

The modulating factor $(1-p_t)^\gamma$ down-weights easy (well-classified) examples, forcing the network to focus on hard, malignant samples.

**Step 5 — Performance Metric (1 mark)**
- **AUROC = 0.96**, **Sensitivity = 89.4%**, **Specificity = 91.2%**.
- Comparison to dermatologists: AI matched the average accuracy of 21 board-certified dermatologists.

**Step 6 — Industrial Impact (1 mark)**
- Deployed as **"SkinVision"** smartphone app, screened **> 1 million users**.
- Provided triage support in primary-care clinics in low-resource regions lacking specialists.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Why Transformers Dominate Industrial NLP (2 marks)**
Transformers replace recurrence with **self-attention**, enabling:
- **Parallelization** across sequence positions (RNNs process sequentially → slow).
- **Long-range dependency modeling** without vanishing gradients.
- The core operation is:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Step 2 — Case Study 1: BERT in Google Search (2 marks)**
- Google replaced its RankBrain RNN with **BERT-based ranking** in 2019.
- Handles **natural-language queries** like "2019 brazil traveller to usa need a visa" — BERT understands the traveller-to-USA directional context.
- Result: Improved search-relevance on **~10% of English queries**.

**Step 3 — Case Study 2: GPT in Customer-Service Chatbots (2 marks)**
- **GPT-3/4 fine-tuned** via RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) powers enterprise chatbots (e.g., Intercom, Drift, Bank of America's Erica).
- Handles multi-turn conversations, summarization, and code generation.
- Reduced live-agent workload by **~40%**, with 24/7 availability.

**Step 4 — Comparative Table (1 mark)**

| Feature | RNN / LSTM | Transformer |
|---------|------------|-------------|
| Sequential computation | Yes (slow) | No (parallel) |
| Long-range dependencies | Limited (gates) | Direct via attention |
| Training cost | Lower for short sequences | Higher but scalable |
| Industrial adoption | Legacy systems | Modern SOTA |

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With a suitable case study, illustrate how **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** are transforming the **pharmaceutical industry**. Explain the architecture, training objective, and one measurable industrial outcome.

**(b)** [7 marks] Describe how **Deep Reinforcement Learning** is used in **industrial robotics and autonomous-driving** systems. Cover the Markov Decision Process formulation and a named industrial deployment.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — Pharmaceutical Problem (1 mark)**
Drug discovery is bottlenecked by a search space of ~$10^{60}$ possible molecules. Wet-lab synthesis is costly and slow (years per drug).

**Step 2 — GAN Architecture for Molecules (2 marks)**
**Insilico Medicine's Molecular GAN** uses:
- **Generator (G)**: Takes a noise vector $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and outputs a molecular graph/SMILES string.
- **Discriminator (D)**: Distinguishes real molecules (from ChEMBL database) from generated ones.
- **Reward shaping** biases generation toward molecules with high predicted binding affinity to a target protein (e.g., DDR1 kinase).

**Step 3 — GAN Training Objective (2 marks)**
The **minimax objective**:

$$
\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]
$$

- $D$ is trained to **maximize** the probability of correctly classifying real vs. fake.
- $G$ is trained to **minimize** $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, i.e., fool the discriminator.

**Step 4 — Industrial Outcome (2 marks)**
- Insilico Medicine identified a novel **DDR1 kinase inhibitor** in **21 days** (vs. years traditionally).
- Advanced to preclinical trials within 18 months; demonstrated ~$10\times$ cost reduction in early-stage discovery.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Industrial Need for RL (1 mark)**
Sequential decision-making problems in robotics and self-driving cars cannot be solved with one-shot supervised learning because actions influence future states.

**Step 2 — Markov Decision Process (MDP) Formulation (2 marks)**
An MDP is defined by the tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $S$: state space (e.g., vehicle position + LIDAR map).
- $A$: action space (steering, acceleration, braking).
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: transition probability.
- $R(s, a)$: reward function (+1 for staying in lane, −10 for collision).
- $\gamma \in [0,1]$: discount factor.

The agent maximizes expected discounted return:

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k}
$$

**Step 3 — Deep RL Algorithm — DQN (2 marks)**
**Deep Q-Network (Mnih et al., 2015)** approximates the action-value function $Q(s, a)$ with a CNN:

$$
Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') \,\Big|\, s_t = s, a_t = a\right]
$$

Training minimizes:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'; \theta^-) - Q(s,a; \theta)\right)^2\right]
$$

where $\theta^-$ are target-network weights updated periodically for stability.

**Step 4 — Industrial Deployment (2 marks)**
- **Waymo's motion planning** uses **Deep RL + Imitation Learning** hybrids; its cars have driven **> 20 million miles** autonomously.
- **OpenAI's Dactyl** robotic hand solved Rubik's cube using **PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)** with domain randomization.
- **Boston Dynamics** combines RL with model-predictive control for dynamic legged locomotion.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on case-study questions**:
>
> 1. **Naming the architecture without justifying it** — stating "CNN is used" earns 0.5 marks; saying "CNN with transfer learning from ImageNet because the medical dataset has only 5 000 samples" earns the full 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the metric** — every case-study answer must end with a **quantitative metric** (AUROC, mAP, BLEU, F1). Without numbers, the answer is incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing GAN objective direction** — many students write the wrong sign on $G$'s objective. Remember: $D$ **maximizes**, $G$ **minimizes** $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.
> 4. **Omitting the business impact** — KTU examiners reward answers that connect the technical result to a **real-world consequence** (cost saved, lives impacted, throughput gained).
> 5. **Writing RNN/Transformer comparison incorrectly** — note that Transformers are *not* a successor only to RNNs in NLP; they also dominate modern **vision (ViT)** and **multimodal (CLIP)** domains.
> 6. **Missing formulas** — in a 14-mark question, write at least **one boxed equation** for full marks in the "derivation" sub-part.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Case Studies of DL in Industries**
>
> ✅ A case study = **Problem → Data → Architecture → Loss → Metric → Impact**.
> ✅ **Healthcare**: CNNs (Inception, U-Net, EfficientNet) dominate imaging; AUROC and IoU are key metrics.
> ✅ **Autonomous Vehicles**: HydraNet, YOLO, PointPillars; mAP@0.5 and real-time latency are critical.
> ✅ **Finance**: Wide&Deep, LSTM, Autoencoders for fraud/anomaly; F1-score and AUC lead.
> ✅ **NLP**: BERT (bidirectional encoder), GPT (autoregressive decoder); BLEU/ROUGE for generation.
> ✅ **Generative AI / Pharma**: GANs, Transformer-based molecule generators; objective is the minimax game.
> ✅ **RL / Robotics**: MDP, DQN, PPO, A3C; reward shaping and discount factor $\gamma$ are key.
> ✅ **Manufacturing**: 1D-CNN + LSTM for sensor time-series; RMSE/MSE for forecasting.
> ✅ **Agriculture**: U-Net for weed/crop segmentation; herbicide reduction up to 77%.
> ✅ **Retail / Recsys**: DeepFM, Two-Tower models; NDCG@k for ranking quality.
> ✅ **Cross-industry success factors**: **Transfer Learning**, **Data Augmentation**, **Domain Adaptation**, **Edge Deployment**, **MLOps**, **Model Interpretability (Grad-CAM, SHAP)**.
> ✅ Always **quote a number** in the answer (accuracy %, cost saved, dataset size).
> ✅ Always **state the loss function** and **justify the architecture choice**.
